items.title: Kubernetes v1.35: New level of efficiency with in-place Pod restart
items.content:
The release of Kubernetes 1.35 introduces a powerful new feature that provides a much-requested capability: the ability to trigger a full, in-place restart of the Pod. This feature, (alpha in 1.35), allows for an efficient way to reset a Pod's state compared to resource-intensive approach of deleting and recreating the entire Pod. This feature is especially useful for AI/ML workloads allowing application developers to concentrate on their core training logic while offloading complex failure-handling and recovery mechanisms to sidecars and declarative Kubernetes configuration. With and other planned enhancements, Kubernetes continues to add building blocks for creating the most flexible, robust, and efficient platforms for AI/ML workloads.

This new functionality is available by enabling the RestartAllContainersOnContainerExits feature gate. This alpha feature extends the feature, which graduated to beta in Kubernetes 1.35.

The problem: when a single container restart isn't enough and recreating pods is too costly

Kubernetes has long supported restart policies at the Pod level () and, more recently, at the individual container level. These policies are great for handling crashes in a single, isolated process. However, many modern applications have more complex inter-container dependencies. For instance:

  • An prepares the environment by mounting a volume or generating a configuration file. If the main application container corrupts this environment, simply restarting that one container is not enough. The entire initialization process needs to run again.
  • A monitors system health. If it detects an unrecoverable but retriable error state, it must trigger a restart of the main application container from a clean slate.
  • A that manages a remote resource fails. Even if the sidecar restarts on its own, the main container may be stuck trying to access an outdated or broken connection.

In all these cases, the desired action is not to restart a single container, but all of them. Previously, the only way to achieve this was to delete the Pod and have a controller (like a Job or ReplicaSet) create a new one. This process is slow and expensive, involving the scheduler, node resource allocation and re-initialization of networking and storage.

This inefficiency becomes even worse when handling large-scale AI/ML workloads (>= 1,000 Nodes with one Pod per Node). A common requirement for these synchronous workloads is that when a failure occurs (such as a Node crash), all Pods in the fleet must be recreated to reset the state before training can resume, even if all the other Pods were not directly affected by the failure. Deleting, creating and scheduling thousands of Pods simultaneously creates a massive bottleneck. The estimated overhead of this failure could cost $100,000 per month in wasted resources.

Handling these failures for AI/ML training jobs requires a complex integration touching both the training framework and Kubernetes, which are often fragile and toilsome. This feature introduces a Kubernetes-native solution, improving system robustness and allowing application developers to concentrate on their core training logic.

Another major benefit of restarting Pods in place is that keeping Pods on their assigned Nodes allows for further optimizations. For example, one can implement node-level caching tied to a specific Pod identity, something that is impossible when Pods are unnecessarily being recreated on different Nodes.

Introducing the action

To address this, Kubernetes v1.35 adds a new action to the container restart rules: . When a container exits in a way that matches a rule with this action, the kubelet initiates a fast, restart of the Pod.

This in-place restart is highly efficient because it preserves the Pod's most important resources:

  • The Pod's UID, IP address and network namespace.
  • The Pod's sandbox and any attached devices.
  • All volumes, including and mounted volumes from PVCs.

After terminating all running containers, the Pod's startup sequence is re-executed from the very beginning. This means all are run again in order, followed by the sidecar and regular containers, ensuring a completely fresh start in a known-good environment. With the exception of ephemeral containers (which are terminated), all other containers—including those that previously succeeded or failed—will be restarted, regardless of their individual restart policies.

1. Efficient restarts for ML/Batch jobs

With actions you can address this by enabling a much faster, hybrid recovery strategy: recreate only the "bad" Pods (e.g., those on unhealthy Nodes) while triggering for the remaining healthy Pods. Benchmarks show this reduces the recovery overhead from minutes to a few seconds.

With in-place restarts, a watcher sidecar can monitor the main training process. If it encounters a specific, retriable error, the watcher can exit with a designated code to trigger a fast reset of the worker Pod, allowing it to restart from the last checkpoint without involving the Job controller. This capability is now natively supported by Kubernetes.

2. Re-running init containers for a clean state

Imagine a scenario where an init container is responsible for fetching credentials or setting up a shared volume. If the main application fails in a way that corrupts this shared state, you need the init container to rerun.

By configuring the main application to exit with a specific code upon detecting such a corruption, you can trigger the action, guaranteeing that the init container provides a clean setup before the application restarts.

3. Handling high rate of similar tasks execution

There are cases when tasks are best represented as a Pod execution. And each task requires a clean execution. The task may be a game session backend or some queue item processing. If the rate of tasks is high, running the whole cycle of Pod creation, scheduling and initialization is simply too expensive, especially when tasks can be short. The ability to restart all containers from scratch enables a Kubernetes-native way to handle this scenario without custom solutions or frameworks.

To try this feature, you must enable the RestartAllContainersOnContainerExits feature gate on your Kubernetes cluster components (API server and kubelet) running Kubernetes v1.35+. This alpha feature extends the feature, which graduated to beta in v1.35 and is enabled by default.

Once enabled, you can add to any container (init, sidecar, or regular) and use the action.

The feature is designed to be easily usable on existing apps. However, if an application does not follow some best practices, it may cause issues for the application or for observability tooling. When enabling the feature, make sure that all containers are reentrant and that external tooling is prepared for init containers to re-run. Also, when restarting all containers, the kubelet does not run hooks. This means containers must be designed to handle abrupt termination without relying on hooks for graceful shutdown.

To make this process observable, a new Pod condition, , is added to the Pod's status. When a restart is triggered, this condition becomes and it reverts to once all containers have terminated and the Pod is ready to start its lifecycle anew. This provides a clear signal to users and other cluster components about the Pod's state.

All containers restarted by this action will have their restart count incremented in the container status.

As an alpha feature, is ready for you to experiment with and any use cases and feedback are welcome. This feature is driven by the SIG Node community. If you are interested in getting involved, sharing your thoughts, or contributing, please join us!

You can reach SIG Node through:

items.title: Digest #195: Tech in 2026, SRE Agent with Claude and Kubernetes, CI/CD for Monorepo and Free DevOps Certificates
items.author: Mohamed Labouardy
items.content:
Welcome to this week’s edition of the DevOps Bulletin and happy New Year 2026 🎉

This week: AWS’s CTO share his predictions for 2026 and beyond, why developers are gaming GitHub profiles (and why stars don’t mean what they used to), and an honest reflection on year eight as a founder CTO. We also look at the production bug that made one engineer finally care about undefined behavior, and how Decathlon switched from Spark to Polars to speed up data pipelines while cutting infrastructure costs.

On the hands-on side: building an autonomous monitoring agent with Claude and Kubernetes, mapping real-world initial access vectors into AWS environments, designing CI/CD pipelines for monorepos, organizing cloud spend with AWS cost allocation tags, monitoring a Docker homelab, understanding memory allocation in Go, why clock synchronization is harder than it looks, and building centralized alerting across an AWS organization with CloudWatch, EventBridge, Lambda, and CDK.

This week’s video is a blunt reflection on why 2025 was rough for software engineers: a brutal job market, rising costs, endless AI tools that don’t actually improve software quality, supply-chain breaches becoming routine, and a growing gap between “AI hype” and real developer productivity.

Open-source picks this week include ZITADEL, an open-source identity and access management platform; SerenityOS, a from-scratch Unix-like operating system with its own kernel and desktop; SeaQuery, a type-safe Rust library for building SQL queries; a community-maintained list of free cloud and DevOps certifications; and Ensue, a persistent memory system for AI tools.

FinOps tip of the week: many AWS services ship with safe but expensive defaults. Fix log retention, storage tiers, and cross-AZ traffic once, then bake the right defaults directly into your Terraform templates.

All this and more in this week’s DevOps Bulletin, don’t miss out!

Consider supporting it with a paid subscription. You’ll keep the free Friday issues get extras like bonus deep-dives, templates, and the full archive.

Many AWS services ship with safe but expensive defaults. Log retention, storage tiers, and cross AZ traffic are often set to safe but expensive values. Fix them once, then bake the right defaults into your Terraform templates.

If you want more hands-on tips like this, check out my latest book, “.

  • is an open source identity and access management platform for authentication, authorization, and user management.

  • A from scratch like operating system with its own kernel, desktop environment, browser, and developer tools, inspired by classic 90s interfaces.

  • is a Rust library for building SQL queries programmatically using a type safe and ergonomic API, with support for MySQL, Postgres, and SQLite.

  • A community maintained list of and certification programs across cloud, DevOps, security, data, and software engineering.

  • is a persistent memory system for AI tools that stores your knowledge, preferences, and decisions across conversations.

If you have feedback to share or are interested in sponsoring this newsletter, feel free to reach out via or simply reply to this email.

items.title: TensorFlow Extended (TFX): Production ML Pipelines for Python
items.author: Sam Austin
items.content:
Your model works perfectly in your Jupyter notebook. Then your manager asks you to put it in production, retrain it monthly, monitor for data drift, and handle edge cases gracefully. Suddenly you’re drowning in infrastructure code — data validation, preprocessing pipelines, model versioning, serving infrastructure, monitoring systems. Six months later, you’re maintaining more plumbing code than actual ML code, and you’re wondering if there’s a better way.

I learned TFX the hard way on a project that went from “prototype” to “critical production system” in three months. We spent weeks building custom pipelines, validation, and monitoring before discovering TFX does all of it — and does it better. TFX is Google’s answer to production ML, battle-tested on systems serving billions of predictions. It’s overcomplicated for prototypes but invaluable for real production systems.

Let me show you what TFX actually does and when it’s worth the steep learning curve.

What Is TFX and Why It Exists

TensorFlow Extended (TFX) is an end-to-end platform for deploying production ML pipelines. It’s not a training library — it’s infrastructure for everything around training.

  • Data validation and statistics
  • Feature engineering at scale
  • Training and hyperparameter tuning
  • Model analysis and validation
  • Model serving and deployment
  • A replacement for TensorFlow/Keras
  • Easy to learn (it’s complex)
  • Necessary for small projects
  • The only way to do production ML

Think of TFX as Kubernetes for ML pipelines — powerful, scalable, and way more complicated than you need until you really need it.

When You Actually Need TFX

Before diving in, understand when TFX makes sense:

  • Models retrain regularly (weekly/monthly)
  • Multiple models in production
  • Team size > 5 ML engineers
  • Handling terabytes of data
  • Strict validation requirements
  • Need reproducible pipelines
  • Scale matters (millions of predictions)
  • Prototyping or experimenting
  • Using simpler alternatives works

I’ve seen teams waste months implementing TFX for a single model that retrains quarterly. That’s overkill. TFX pays off at scale, not for toy projects.

Core TFX Components (The Building Blocks)

TFX is modular. You use the components you need:

ExampleGen: Data Ingestion

Ingests and splits data into train/eval sets:

from tfx.components import CsvExampleGen
# Ingest CSV dataexamples = CsvExampleGen(input_base='data/')

Supports CSV, TFRecord, BigQuery, and custom formats. Handles data splitting automatically.

StatisticsGen: Data Analysis

Generates statistics about your data:

from tfx.components import StatisticsGen
# Generate statisticsstatistics = StatisticsGen(examples=examples.outputs['examples'])

Computes distributions, missing values, correlations — everything you’d do in exploratory analysis, but automated and scalable.

Infers schema from statistics:

from tfx.components import SchemaGen
# Infer schemaschema = SchemaGen(statistics=statistics.outputs['statistics'])

Defines expected data types, ranges, and distributions. This becomes your data contract.

ExampleValidator: Data Validation

Validates new data against schema:

from tfx.components import ExampleValidator
# Validate datavalidator = ExampleValidator(
    statistics=statistics.outputs['statistics'],
    schema=schema.outputs['schema']

Catches data drift, anomalies, and schema violations before training. This alone justifies TFX for critical systems.

Transform: Feature Engineering

Applies transformations consistently:

from tfx.components import Transform
# Define preprocessingtransform = Transform(
    examples=examples.outputs['examples'],
    schema=schema.outputs['schema'],
    module_file='preprocessing.py'
)

The preprocessing logic applies identically during training and serving — no train/serve skew. This is huge.

Trains your TensorFlow model:

from tfx.components import Trainer
# Train modeltrainer = Trainer(    examples=transform.outputs['transformed_examples'],
    schema=schema.outputs['schema'],
    transform_graph=transform.outputs['transform_graph'],
    train_args={'num_steps': 1000},
    eval_args={'num_steps': 100}

Integrates with TensorBoard, supports distributed training, handles checkpointing.

Evaluator: Model Validation

Validates trained model performance:

from tfx.components import Evaluator
# Evaluate modelevaluator = Evaluator(
    examples=examples.outputs['examples'],
    model=trainer.outputs['model'],
    baseline_model=previous_model  # Compare to production model
)

Prevents bad models from reaching production. Compares new models to baselines automatically.

Deploys validated models:

from tfx.components import Pusher
# Deploy modelpusher = Pusher(
    model=trainer.outputs['model'],
    model_blessing=evaluator.outputs['blessing'],  # Only deploys if blessed
    push_destination={'filesystem': {'base_directory': '/serving'}}
)

Only deploys if model passes validation. Supports TensorFlow Serving, AI Platform, and custom deployment targets.

Building Your First TFX Pipeline

Let’s build a complete pipeline:

Step 1: Define Preprocessing

# preprocessing.pyimport tensorflow as tf
import tensorflow_transform as tft
def preprocessing_fn(inputs):    """Preprocessing function for Transform component."""    outputs['age'] = tft.scale_to_z_score(inputs['age'])
    outputs['income'] = tft.scale_to_z_score(inputs['income'])
    outputs['category_idx'] = tft.compute_and_apply_vocabulary(        vocab_filename='category_vocab'    outputs['age_income_cross'] = tft.hash_strings(            tf.strings.as_string(inputs['age']),
            tf.strings.as_string(inputs['income'])
        ], separator='_')    outputs['label'] = inputs['label']

This preprocessing applies identically during training and serving.

# model.pyimport tensorflow as tf
import tensorflow_transform as tft
from tensorflow_transform.tf_metadata import schema_utils
def _build_keras_model():    """Build Keras model."""        'age': tf.keras.Input(shape=(1,), name='age'),
        'income': tf.keras.Input(shape=(1,), name='income'),
        'category_idx': tf.keras.Input(shape=(1,), name='category_idx', dtype=tf.int64)    x = tf.keras.layers.concatenate([        inputs['income'],
        tf.keras.layers.Embedding(input_dim=1000, output_dim=8)(inputs['category_idx'])
    ])    x = tf.keras.layers.Dense(64, activation='relu')(x)
    x = tf.keras.layers.Dropout(0.5)(x)
    x = tf.keras.layers.Dense(32, activation='relu')(x)    outputs = tf.keras.layers.Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')(x)
    model = tf.keras.Model(inputs=inputs, outputs=outputs)
        optimizer='adam',
        loss='binary_crossentropy',
        metrics=['accuracy']
def run_fn(fn_args):    """Training function called by Trainer."""    tf_transform_output = tft.TFTransformOutput(fn_args.transform_output)
    # Create train/eval datasets
    train_dataset = _input_fn(        tf_transform_output,    )
    eval_dataset = _input_fn(
        fn_args.eval_files,        batch_size=32    model = _build_keras_model()        train_dataset,
        validation_data=eval_dataset,
        epochs=10,            tf.keras.callbacks.TensorBoard(log_dir=fn_args.model_run_dir)    )    signatures = {
        'serving_default': _get_serve_tf_examples_fn(model, tf_transform_output)
    }
    model.save(fn_args.serving_model_dir, save_format='tf', signatures=signatures)
def _input_fn(file_pattern, tf_transform_output, batch_size=32):    """Create input dataset."""
    dataset = tf.data.experimental.make_batched_features_dataset(
        file_pattern=file_pattern,        features=tf_transform_output.transformed_feature_spec(),    )
def _get_serve_tf_examples_fn(model, tf_transform_output):    """Create serving signature."""    def serve_tf_examples_fn(serialized_tf_examples):
        feature_spec = tf_transform_output.raw_feature_spec()
        parsed_features = tf.io.parse_example(serialized_tf_examples, feature_spec)
        transformed_features = tf_transform_output.transform_raw_features(parsed_features)
        return model(transformed_features)
    return serve_tf_examples_fn
# pipeline.pyfrom tfx import v1 as tfx
from tfx.orchestration import metadata, pipeline
from tfx.orchestration.local.local_dag_runner import LocalDagRunner
def create_pipeline(    pipeline_name: str,    data_root: str,    serving_model_dir: str,):
    """Creates TFX pipeline."""
    example_gen = tfx.components.CsvExampleGen(input_base=data_root)    statistics_gen = tfx.components.StatisticsGen(
        examples=example_gen.outputs['examples']
    )    schema_gen = tfx.components.SchemaGen(
        statistics=statistics_gen.outputs['statistics']
    )    example_validator = tfx.components.ExampleValidator(
        statistics=statistics_gen.outputs['statistics'],
        schema=schema_gen.outputs['schema']    transform = tfx.components.Transform(
        examples=example_gen.outputs['examples'],
        schema=schema_gen.outputs['schema'],    )    trainer = tfx.components.Trainer(        examples=transform.outputs['transformed_examples'],
        schema=schema_gen.outputs['schema'],
        transform_graph=transform.outputs['transform_graph'],
        train_args=tfx.proto.TrainArgs(num_steps=1000),
        eval_args=tfx.proto.EvalArgs(num_steps=100)    evaluator = tfx.components.Evaluator(
        examples=example_gen.outputs['examples'],
        model=trainer.outputs['model']    pusher = tfx.components.Pusher(
        model=trainer.outputs['model'],
        model_blessing=evaluator.outputs['blessing'],
        push_destination=tfx.proto.PushDestination(
            filesystem=tfx.proto.PushDestination.Filesystem(
                base_directory=serving_model_dir
            )    )        example_gen,        schema_gen,        transform,        evaluator,    ]
    return pipeline.Pipeline(
        pipeline_name=pipeline_name,
        pipeline_root=pipeline_root,
        components=components,
        metadata_connection_config=metadata.sqlite_metadata_connection_config(
            metadata_path    )
if __name__ == '__main__':    # Create and run pipeline
    tfx_pipeline = create_pipeline(
        pipeline_name='my_pipeline',
        pipeline_root='./pipeline_output',
        data_root='./data',        serving_model_dir='./serving_model',
        metadata_path='./metadata.db'
    )
    LocalDagRunner().run(tfx_pipeline)

This creates a complete production pipeline with validation, training, and deployment.

TFX with Apache Beam (Distributed Processing)

For large-scale data processing, use Apache Beam:

from tfx.orchestration.beam.beam_dag_runner import BeamDagRunner
# Run pipeline with BeamBeamDagRunner().run(tfx_pipeline)

This scales preprocessing and validation to massive datasets using distributed computing.

TFX with Kubeflow (Kubernetes Orchestration)

For Kubernetes environments:

from tfx.orchestration.kubeflow import kubeflow_dag_runner
# Configure Kubeflowrunner_config = kubeflow_dag_runner.KubeflowDagRunnerConfig(
    kubeflow_metadata_config=kubeflow_dag_runner.KubeflowMetadataConfig(),
    tfx_image='tensorflow/tfx:latest'
# Run on Kubeflowkubeflow_dag_runner.KubeflowDagRunner(config=runner_config).run(tfx_pipeline)

Let me be brutally honest about TFX’s downsides:

  • Documentation assumes expertise
    • More code than simple alternatives
    • Requires infrastructure knowledge
    • Overkill for simple projects

    I’ve seen teams spend three months implementing TFX for a model that could have been deployed with Flask in a week. That’s wasteful. IMO, TFX makes sense only when you have multiple production models or complex pipelines requiring strong validation and monitoring.

    Before committing to TFX, consider simpler alternatives:

          • Better developer experience

          For many teams, MLflow + simple deployment handles 90% of production needs with 10% of TFX’s complexity.

          Despite the complexity, TFX excels in specific scenarios:

          • Running 10+ production models
          • Data validation is critical
          • Processing terabytes of data
          • Need reproducible pipelines
          • Already using TensorFlow heavily
          • Scale justifies complexity

          Real TFX success stories:

          • Google (obviously — they built it)
          • Twitter (recommendation systems)
          • Spotify (music recommendations)
          • Large enterprises with dedicated ML platform teams

          Notice the pattern? Large scale, dedicated teams, critical systems. Not startups or small teams.

          TFX is industrial-strength production ML infrastructure. It’s powerful, scalable, and battle-tested at Google scale. It’s also complex, opinionated, and overkill for most projects.

          • Already invested in TensorFlow
          • Building ML platform for multiple teams
          • Prototyping or experimenting
          • Don’t have ML platform expertise

          Most teams should start simpler (MLflow, basic deployment) and graduate to TFX only when hitting real limitations. The complexity overhead is only justified at scale.

          But honestly, if you’re just trying TFX out of curiosity, you’ll probably abandon it after a week. TFX requires commitment — architectural decisions, team buy-in, infrastructure support. It’s not a library you casually try on a weekend project.

          If you genuinely need production ML pipelines at scale with strong validation and monitoring, TFX is excellent. For everything else, simpler alternatives work better. Don’t let Google’s marketing convince you that TFX is necessary for production ML. It’s one option — a powerful one at the right scale, but often overkill.

          Now stop over-engineering your ML deployment and start with whatever gets your model serving predictions reliably. You can always graduate to TFX later when you actually need it. Most teams never do. :)

items.title: Latency-Critical Core Algorithms for Building Predictable, High-Throughput In-Memory Systems
items.author: Scaibu
items.content:
Core algorithms that prevent latency spikes and memory blowups in single-threaded, in-memory systems: hash tables with incremental rehashing, cache-efficient collision handling, compact encodings, quicklist, and SDS — essential for predictable performance at scale as in .

This document focuses on the non-negotiable algorithms required when latency spikes, memory waste, or blocking operations are unacceptable. It specifically explains how hash tables provide constant-time access, why incremental rehashing is mandatory to prevent event-loop stalls during growth, and how minimizes pointer chasing and cache misses under high request rates.

It then addresses the faced by real workloads: small keys, short strings, and integer-heavy collections that would otherwise explode RAM usage. Ziplist, listpack, and intset are examined as that reduce memory overhead by orders of magnitude, while quicklist demonstrates how to preserve fast insert/delete semantics without sacrificing cache locality. Finally, SDS is presented as a , not an optimization, for safe and amortized-O(1) string manipulation under continuous mutation.

These algorithms are not optional improvements; they are for single-threaded, in-memory systems such as operating at scale, where a single blocking resize, cache-inefficient structure, or unsafe string operation can immediately translate into user-visible latency, throughput collapse, or system instability.

Modern in-memory data systems must satisfy a demanding and often contradictory set of constraints: extremely low latency, high throughput, predictable performance under load, efficient memory utilization, and operational simplicity. These constraints are magnified when the system is single-threaded and event-driven, where any blocking operation directly translates into visible service degradation. The algorithms listed here — hash tables, incremental rehashing, open addressing, compact encodings (ziplist, listpack, intset), quicklist, and SDS — are not isolated data structures. They are interlocking design decisions that together define how such a system survives real-world workloads.

At the core lies the hash table (dictionary) algorithm, which provides average O(1) access for key–value operations and serves as the backbone for indexing, metadata management, and primary data access. However, a naive hash table implementation fails under dynamic workloads where key cardinality fluctuates. This leads directly to the need for incremental rehashing, a strategy that spreads resize cost across many operations to preserve latency guarantees in a single-threaded execution model. Open addressing further refines this foundation by handling collisions without pointer-heavy chaining, improving cache locality and reducing memory overhead — both critical for in-memory systems.

On top of this foundational layer, the system must store heterogeneous data efficiently. Real-world data is often small, sparse, or integer-heavy, and storing it using general-purpose structures wastes memory and harms cache behavior. Ziplist and its successor listpack address this by using contiguous, compact encodings optimized for small collections and short strings. Intset specializes further, providing a space-efficient, integer-only set representation that avoids hashing entirely when possible. Quicklist then reconciles competing requirements by combining linked-list semantics with ziplist/listpack compression, enabling fast insertions while retaining memory locality.

Finally, SDS (Simple Dynamic String) provides a fundamental abstraction for string handling that avoids the pitfalls of C-style strings, such as repeated length scans and buffer overflows, while still enabling amortized O(1) append operations. SDS underpins nearly every higher-level structure, ensuring predictable string manipulation performance across the system.

Taken together, these algorithms form a layered, adaptive architecture. Each structure is selected not for theoretical elegance alone, but for how it behaves under operational stress: skewed access patterns, mixed workloads, memory pressure, and the need for deterministic latency. Understanding them as a unified design is essential to understanding how systems like achieve their performance characteristics.

Redis dict — two-table incremental rehashing for predictable operation latency

Problem Statement (expanded)

Redis is a single-threaded, in-memory key–value server that must sustain very high throughput and strict per-command latency guarantees while keys grow and shrink unpredictably. A conventional hash table resize requires allocating a larger bucket array and rehashing every existing entry in a single blocking operation. That operation costs O(N) time where N is the number of entries; in a single-threaded event loop such a full rehash will stall the server and produce unacceptable latency spikes.

The requirement is to enable table growth and shrinkage while simultaneously:

  • avoiding long, blocking pauses in the event loop;
  • maintaining correctness of reads and writes throughout resizing;
  • preserving average O(1) access time and keeping per-operation latency practically bounded;
  • allowing growth and shrink based on load without losing entries or returning stale results.

The approach is to split the resize work into many small, bounded migration steps executed alongside normal operations, preserving correctness and responsiveness.

  1. The dictionary provides two internal table slots: ht0 and ht1. When no resize is active, ht1 is NULL and rehash_idx equals -1.
  2. rehash_idx is the next bucket index in ht0 to migrate. rehash_idx == -1 signals no active migration.
  3. Entries not yet migrated always reside in ht0. Migrated entries reside in ht1. No entry is duplicated across tables.
  4. Lookups during migration consult both tables; migrated items will be found in ht1.
  5. Mutations performed while migrating insert into ht1 to avoid repeated migration of newly added keys.
  6. Migration moves entire bucket chains (linked lists) atomically per bucket index.
  7. Rehash completes when all buckets from ht0 have been processed and ht0.used == 0; then ht1 becomes the new ht0 and rehash_idx resets to -1.

System-level reasoning and trade-offs

Distributing the resize work into many small steps converts a single large, blocking cost into many bounded costs, preserving server responsiveness at the expense of slightly higher average per-operation work while resizing. Coexistence of two tables raises peak memory usage during migration. Lookups during migration may consult two tables, increasing per-lookup work until migration completes. The migration granularity (buckets per step) and the thresholds that trigger resizing are tunable parameters that balance memory footprint, throughput, and per-command latency. A robust hash function is required to avoid pathological chain growth; if adversarial keys are a concern, additional bucket-structure strategies can be adopted.

Incremental rehashing algorithm(radis)

Redis is a single-threaded, in-memory key–value server that must sustain very high throughput while keeping per-command latency tightly bounded. A conventional hash-table resize (allocate a larger bucket array and rehash every entry in one blocking pass) is O(N) work and will stall a single-threaded event loop long enough to cause visible service pauses. The requirement is to allow table growth and shrinkage while simultaneously:

  • avoiding long, blocking pauses in the event loop;
  • preserving correctness of reads and writes during the entire resize window;
  • keeping average access time at O(1) and per-command latency practically bounded;
  • allowing automatic resizing based on load without data loss or stale results.

Redis addresses this by performing the resize as a sequence of small, bounded migration steps executed alongside normal operations rather than as a single monolithic rehash. The production implementation uses a pair of internal hash tables and moves bucket chains from the old table to the new table incrementally.

Core invariants and guarantees

  1. Two-table representation: the dictionary exposes two internal table slots, ht0 and ht1. Normally ht1 is NULL. When resize begins, ht1 is allocated and rehash_idx is set to the first bucket to migrate. Gist
  2. Rehash pointer: rehash_idx points to the next bucket index in ht0 to process; rehash_idx == -1 means no rehash is active.
  3. Single residence: any entry not yet migrated lives only in ht0; migrated entries live only in ht1. No key exists simultaneously in both tables.
  4. Bucket-granularity moves: migration moves entire bucket chains (linked lists) at a time; chains are inserted into ht1 using the new table’s mask. Codeburst
  5. Lookup correctness: while rehashing, lookups must consult both tables (typical implementation checks ht1 first, then ht0).
  6. Mutation policy: inserts and updates during rehash go to ht1 (the receiving table) so freshly inserted keys are not re-migrated.
  7. Termination: rehashing completes when every bucket in ht0 has been processed and ht0.used == 0; then ht1 replaces ht0 and rehash_idx resets to -1. Eric's Blog

  5. Rehash completes when all buckets in ht0 are migrated; then ht1 becomes the active ht0, ht1 is set to NULL, and rehash_idx resets to -1.

System-level rationale and trade-offs

  • Splitting one large O(N) task into many small steps prevents a single long pause and trades a peak latency event for slightly higher per-operation work while rehashing is in progress.
  • Two-table coexistence increases peak memory usage until migration finishes.
  • During rehash, lookups may require checking two tables, which increases average CPU per lookup until migration completes.
  • Bucket-chain moves preserve linked-list integrity; moving whole chains avoids partial-chain inconsistencies. Codeburst
  • Tuning knobs: load-factor thresholds, buckets-per-step, and whether reads contribute to migration determine the balance between memory headroom, throughput, and latency.
  • A strong, non-adversarial hash function is required to avoid pathological chain lengths; if adversarial inputs are possible, consider chain-structure mitigations (treeification or bounded-bucket strategies).

Open addressing collision handling

Problem statement (expanded)

Hash tables map keys to array slots using a hash function. When two distinct keys map to the same slot, a collision occurs. The problem is to resolve collisions so that lookups, inserts, and deletes remain correct and efficient while using contiguous array storage (no per-bucket linked lists). The solution must address:

  • how to find an alternative slot when the primary slot is occupied;
  • how to guarantee termination of searches and insert attempts when the table is not full;
  • how to handle deletions without breaking subsequent searches;
  • how to choose a probe sequence that avoids pathological clustering and works with power-of-two or prime table sizes;
  • how to maintain acceptable performance as load factor increases and when adversarial key distributions exist.

Open addressing resolves collisions by probing other array indices according to a deterministic sequence derived from the key and the probe number. Common probe strategies include linear probing, quadratic probing, and double hashing. Deletion is commonly implemented with a special “deleted” marker (tombstone) or by re-inserting later to remove tombstones. Proper resizing and periodic table rebuilding are necessary to bound probe lengths and control performance.

Core invariants and guarantees

  1. The table is a contiguous array of slots; each slot has a state: EMPTY, OCCUPIED, or DELETED.
  2. A probe sequence is a deterministic function probe(hash, i, size) that returns the ith slot index to check; for every key the sequence must visit every slot eventually if size and probe function are co-prime (or otherwise guaranteed).
  3. find searches in probe order until it finds the key (O(1) expected) or hits an EMPTY slot (termination condition). DELETED slots do not terminate search.
  4. insert searches in probe order and places the new key in the first DELETED or EMPTY slot encountered; if the key already exists, insert updates the value.
  5. delete marks the slot as DELETED (tombstone) so find remains correct; excessive DELETED density requires table rebuild to recover space and shorten probes.
  6. Load-factor thresholds (e.g., grow when used/size > 0.7) ensure expected probe lengths remain bounded; resize triggers a full rebuild that re-inserts OCCUPIED entries into a freshly allocated table, clearing DELETED states.
  7. If double hashing is used, the secondary hash must produce a step value that is non-zero and co-prime with the table size when needed to guarantee full table coverage.

Execution flow (operational sequence)

  1. Create a table with a chosen initial size and pick a probe function (linear, quadratic with constants, or double hashing with a secondary hash).
  2. On insert, check resize condition and rebuild to a larger table if needed; then probe in sequence to find an existing key or the first DELETED/EMPTY slot and place the key there.
  3. On find, probe in sequence until the key is found or an EMPTY slot is reached; DELETED slots are treated as transparent and do not terminate the search.
  4. On delete, mark the located OCCUPIED slot as DELETED, update used and tombstones; if tombstone count grows too large, rebuild the table by reinserting OCCUPIED entries into a fresh table thereby clearing tombstones.
  5. Rebuilds (resizes) reinsert OCCUPIED entries using the same probe strategy into the new array, which clears DELETED markers and reduces probe lengths.

System-level rationale and trade-offs

  • Linear probing offers excellent cache locality and simple arithmetic but suffers from primary clustering, where contiguous groups grow and cause longer probes.
  • Quadratic probing reduces primary clustering but requires careful constant selection and usually table sizes that allow coverage of the table; it avoids long primary clusters but can exhibit secondary clustering.
  • Double hashing provides the best distribution among open-addressing schemes and minimizes clustering by using a second hash to compute step size; it requires the step to be co-prime with table size to guarantee full coverage.
  • Tombstones allow deletions without breaking search correctness but accumulate and degrade performance; periodic table rebuilds remove tombstones at the cost of O(n) work amortized by triggers (e.g., tombstone threshold or load factor adjustments).
  • Open addressing uses less memory per entry than separate chaining (no pointers per element beyond slot storage) and gives good locality, but it is sensitive to high load factors and adversarial key distributions.
  • Choice of table size policy (power-of-two vs. prime) interacts with probe function choice: power-of-two simplifies index arithmetic and bitmasking for linear/quadratic probes; prime sizes pair well with double hashing where modulo arithmetic is used.
  • Robust hash functions are essential to avoid pathological clustering; in adversarial environments consider guarded strategies such as randomized hashing seeds or switching to chaining when probe lengths exceed thresholds.

Ziplist encoding algorithm (Redis)

Problem statement (expanded)

Redis needs a compact, contiguous-memory representation for small lists, hashes and sorted-sets to reduce memory overhead and pointer waste for small elements. The structure must support forward and backward traversal, store mixed strings and integers efficiently, and remain reasonably fast for short collections. The ziplist (a packed, doubly-traversable sequence of entries in a single memory block) was designed to meet that need by using variable-length encodings for entry metadata and payloads so small elements consume minimal bytes. Redis+1

Core invariants and guarantees

  1. A ziplist is a single contiguous byte array with a fixed-format header and a terminating end byte. The header stores total bytes, offset of tail, and number of entries.
  2. Each entry encodes: the length of the entry (prevlen), a compact encoding descriptor for the current entry (type + length), and the payload (string bytes or integer in minimal-width form). Prevlen uses either 1 byte for lengths < 254 or a 5-byte form when the previous entry is ≥254 bytes.
  3. The prevlen field permits backward traversal by allowing the reader to jump to the previous entry without global offsets. Entry encodings are variable-length to minimize overhead for short strings/integers.
  4. Operations that change element sizes may require reallocating the entire ziplist block (insert/delete/resize). For this reason, ziplist is best for small collections where realloc cost is acceptable.
  5. Ziplist encoding has been superseded in newer Redis releases by listpack in many places, but the core compact-encoding ideas and prevlen mechanism are the same. Implementations and thresholds can differ by Redis version.

    new_entry_bytes = prevlen_field + enc_header + payload_bytes
    zipbuf = resize_and_insert(zipbuf, insert_pos, new_entry_bytes)
    header.zlbytes = header.zlbytes + length(new_entry_bytes)
    header.zltail = insert_pos
    header.zllen = header.zllen + 1
    write_header(zipbuf, header)
    RETURN zipbuf
FUNCTION delete_entry_at(zipbuf, entry_offset):
    header = read_header(zipbuf)
    entry_len = read_entry_length(zipbuf, entry_offset)
    next_offset = entry_offset + entry_len
    zipbuf = remove_range(zipbuf, entry_offset, entry_len)
    header.zlbytes = header.zlbytes - entry_len
    header.zllen = header.zllen - 1
    adjust_prevlen_of_next_entry_if_needed(zipbuf, entry_offset)
    write_header(zipbuf, header)
    RETURN zipbuf
FUNCTION read_entry_at(zipbuf, offset):
    (prevlen, prevlen_bytes) = decode_prevlen(zipbuf, offset)
    pos = offset + prevlen_bytes
    (type, payload_len, is_int) = decode_encoding_header(zipbuf, pos)
    pos = pos + length_of_encoding_header(type)
    payload = zipbuf[pos:pos+payload_len]
    RETURN (prevlen, type, payload, prevlen_bytes, payload_len)
FUNCTION iterate(zipbuf):
    header = read_header(zipbuf)
    offset = header_start_size()
    WHILE offset < header.zlbytes - 1:
        (prevlen, type, payload, prevlen_bytes, payload_len) = read_entry_at(zipbuf, offset)
        yield (offset, prevlen, type, payload)
        offset = offset + prevlen_bytes + length_of_encoding_header(type) + payload_len
FUNCTION find_by_index(zipbuf, index):
    header = read_header(zipbuf)        index = header.zllen + index
    offset = header_start_size()
    i = 0
    WHILE i < header.zllen AND offset < header.zlbytes - 1:
        IF i == index:
            RETURN read_entry_at(zipbuf, offset)
        entry_len = read_entry_length(zipbuf, offset)
        offset = offset + entry_len
        i = i + 1

Execution flow (operational sequence)

  1. Create a ziplist buffer with header and a single end byte.
  2. To append: compute prev-entry length from current tail, encode prevlen and payload header, insert the new entry bytes before the end byte, update header fields (zlbytes, zltail, zllen).
  3. To traverse forward: start at first-entry offset (header size), decode prevlen and encoding header, read payload, advance by entry total length; repeat until end byte.
  4. To traverse backward: use zltail offset, decode prevlen from that entry to find previous entry offset, repeat.
  5. To delete or insert in middle: remove or insert byte ranges and then fix the prevlen field of the next entry if its required prevlen encoding size would change (this may cascade and force further adjustments), then update header numbers and tail offset accordingly.
  6. Because insert/delete may change sizes, implementations typically perform a single realloc to resize the whole buffer; this makes operation cost linear in worst case for long lists.

System-level rationale and trade-offs

  • Ziplist minimizes per-element overhead by encoding metadata compactly and co-locating entries. This reduces pointer overhead and improves cache locality for small lists.
  • The prevlen trick enables backward traversal without storing per-entry back-pointers, but requires a variable-length prevlen encoding that complicates inserts/deletes when prevlen field size toggles between 1 and 5 bytes; fixing those cases can cascade and increase work.
  • Ziplist is best for small collections where memory savings outweigh realloc cost. For larger or mutation-heavy collections Redis switches to pointer-based structures (or uses listpack in newer versions) to avoid frequent costly reallocations.
  • The design choices (prevlen thresholds, specific integer encodings, header formats) are implementation details that trade encode/decode complexity for minimized memory size per element; production code uses carefully tuned thresholds and a small set of integer encodings for performance.

Intset encoding algorithm (Redis)

Problem statement (expanded)

Redis requires a compact, contiguous-memory representation for sets of integers that are typically small in cardinality and dominated by integer values. The goals are minimal memory overhead, good cache locality, and reasonably fast membership, insertion and deletion for small collections. The representation must store unique integers, allow forward traversal, and avoid per-element pointers. The difficulty is that integers in practice vary in magnitude, so a fixed-width encoding either wastes space (if chosen large) or fails to hold some values (if chosen small). The problem is to design an encoding that:

  • stores all elements in a contiguous array with no per-element allocation;
  • maintains uniqueness and sorted order for efficient search;
  • uses the smallest fixed-width integer encoding that can represent every element currently stored;
  • supports adding a value that requires a larger encoding by promoting the entire array to a wider format;
  • keeps operations simple and deterministic in a single-threaded runtime.

Redis’ intset solves this by keeping a single homogeneous integer-width array (16-, 32- or 64-bit elements), storing values in sorted order, and performing an atomic promotion of the whole array when an inserted value does not fit the current width.

Core invariants and guarantees

  1. The structure stores a header containing the current element width (encoding) and element count (length) followed by a contiguous array of length integers encoded using encoding.
  2. All elements use the same signed integer width: 16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit.
  3. Elements are strictly ordered (sorted ascending) and unique.
  4. Membership uses binary search over the encoded array; complexity O(log n).
  5. Insert and delete maintain sorted order by shifting suffix elements; complexity O(n) in the worst case.
  6. If a new value cannot be represented in the current encoding, the entire array is promoted to the smallest encoding that can hold the new value and existing values; promotion is O(n) and preserves order.
  7. No per-element pointers or metadata beyond the compact header exist; memory footprint per element is minimal for the chosen encoding.
CONST ENC_INT16 = 2CONST ENC_INT32 = 4    encoding    contents  // array of raw bytes interpreted as signed integers of width `encoding`
FUNCTION min_encoding_for_value(v):
    IF v >= -32768 AND v <= 32767:    IF v >= -2147483648 AND v <= 2147483647:    RETURN ENC_INT64
FUNCTION element_at(intset, index):
    start = index * intset.encoding
    RETURN decode_signed_integer(intset.contents[start : start + intset.encoding], intset.encoding)
FUNCTION set_element_at(intset, index, value):
    start = index * intset.encoding
    bytes = encode_signed_integer(value, intset.encoding)
    intset.contents[start : start + intset.encoding] = bytes
FUNCTION allocate_contents(count, encoding):
    RETURN byte_array_of_length(count * encoding)
FUNCTION create_intset(initial_encoding, initial_capacity):
    s = new Intset
    s.encoding = initial_encoding
    s.length = 0
    s.contents = allocate_contents(initial_capacity, initial_encoding)
    RETURN s
FUNCTION binary_search(intset, value):
    low = 0    WHILE low <= high:        midval = element_at(intset, mid)            RETURN (TRUE, mid)            low = mid + 1            high = mid - 1
FUNCTION promote_contents(old_intset, new_encoding):
    new_contents = allocate_contents(old_intset.length, new_encoding)    WHILE i < old_intset.length:
        v = element_at(old_intset, i)
        bytes = encode_signed_integer(v, new_encoding)
        new_contents[i * new_encoding : (i+1) * new_encoding] = bytes
        i = i + 1    new_intset.encoding = new_encoding
    new_intset.length = old_intset.length
    new_intset.contents = new_contents
FUNCTION resize_contents(intset, new_length):
    new_contents = allocate_contents(new_length, intset.encoding)
    copy_bytes(intset.contents, 0, new_contents, 0, intset.length * intset.encoding)
    intset.contents = new_contents
FUNCTION insert_at_index(intset, index, value):
    new_length = intset.length + 1
    intset = resize_contents(intset, new_length)
    i = new_length - 1        v = element_at(intset, i - 1)
        set_element_at(intset, i, v)
        i = i - 1
    set_element_at(intset, index, value)
    intset.length = intset.length + 1
FUNCTION intset_add(intset, value):
    req_enc = min_encoding_for_value(value)
    IF req_enc > intset.encoding:
        intset = promote_contents(intset, req_enc)
    (found, pos) = binary_search(intset, value)
    IF found:    intset = insert_at_index(intset, pos, value)
FUNCTION delete_at_index(intset, index):
    i = index
    WHILE i < intset.length - 1:
        v = element_at(intset, i + 1)
        set_element_at(intset, i, v)
        i = i + 1
    intset.length = intset.length - 1
    RETURN intset
FUNCTION intset_remove(intset, value):
    (found, pos) = binary_search(intset, value)        RETURN (intset, FALSE)
    intset = delete_at_index(intset, pos)
    RETURN (intset, TRUE)
FUNCTION intset_contains(intset, value):
    (found, _) = binary_search(intset, value)
FUNCTION intset_len(intset):
    RETURN intset.length

Execution flow (operational sequence)

  1. Creation: allocate an Intset with a chosen initial encoding (commonly 16-bit) and zero length.
  2. Membership check: call intset_contains, which uses binary_search over the sorted array; return true if found.
  3. Insert: compute minimal encoding for the new value via min_encoding_for_value. If the required encoding is larger than current intset.encoding, call promote_contents to allocate a new contents buffer at the larger width and convert every existing element. Then binary_search returns the insertion index; if the value exists return without change; otherwise insert_at_index shifts the suffix elements right (by copying values element-by-element at the current encoding width) and writes the new value. Update length.
  4. Delete: locate element with binary_search; if found call delete_at_index which shifts the suffix left to overwrite the removed element and decrement length.
  5. Promotion cost: promotion is O(n) due to copying and re-encoding every element. Searches are O(log n). Inserts and deletes require O(n) worst-case due to shifting.
  6. No automatic downcast step is performed in this pseudocode; lowering the encoding would require scanning all elements and reallocating to a narrower encoding only when all values fit, which is optional and rarely done in production.

System-level rationale and trade-offs

  • Memory efficiency: homogeneous fixed-width contiguous storage minimizes per-element overhead and yields excellent cache locality for small sets.
  • Performance: binary search gives fast membership tests, but insert/delete are linear due to shifts. This is acceptable for small sets that are read-dominant or have low mutation rates.
  • Promotion cost: inserting a single large integer may trigger a full-array promotion (O(n)). This cost is unavoidable if the design prioritizes compactness over per-insert worst-case work.
  • Simplicity: the data structure is simple, deterministic and aligns well with Redis’ single-threaded design.
  • Applicability: well-suited for small integer sets (e.g., small sets returned by commands, small sorted-set encodings); not suited for large sets with frequent mutations.
  • Optional policies: downcasting (shrinking encoding) can reclaim memory after deletions but requires scanning all elements to ensure safe narrowing; a practical implementation triggers this rarely to avoid repeated oscillation.
  • Robustness: relies on correct, consistent integer encoding/decoding; choose clear signed-range checks to avoid overflow bugs.SDS (Simple Dynamic String) algorithm

Problem statement (expanded)

Redis requires a simple, efficient, and safe dynamic string abstraction for internal use that:

  • stores binary-safe strings with a terminating zero byte for C-compatibility;
  • provides O(1) length access without scanning the buffer;
  • supports cheap append/concat operations with amortized growth;
  • avoids frequent reallocs by using a growth policy that balances memory and CPU;
  • preserves compactness for small strings while scaling to large values;
  • exposes simple operations for trimming, slicing, copying and comparing.

The SDS design provides a tiny header plus a contiguous byte buffer that stores the string data and a terminating \0. The header holds length and allocation metadata so operations can be implemented with clear, bounded behaviors and without hidden linear-time costs for common length or append operations.

Core invariants and guarantees

  1. SDS is a contiguous block composed of an explicit header and a byte buffer that is always NUL-terminated.
  2. The header stores the current string length (len) and the total allocated buffer size (alloc). The invariant 0 <= len <= alloc holds and buf[len] == 0.
  3. len is returned in O(1) time; obtaining available space is alloc - len.
  4. Append/concat operations ensure there is enough room before copying bytes; if not, SDS grows according to a deterministic growth policy that guarantees amortized O(1) append cost.
  5. All operations preserve binary-safety: the buffer may contain zero bytes inside the payload but a terminating zero is always present after the payload.
  6. Growth uses a preallocation policy that avoids pathological repeated reallocs for moderate growth while keeping headroom reasonable for very large strings.
  7. Memory is owned by the SDS instance; duplication produces an independent SDS; freeing releases the allocated block.
CONST SDS_MAX_PREALLOC = 1024 * 1024    len       // integer: current payload length
    alloc     // integer: allocated buffer size (capacity)
    buf       // byte array of length alloc + 1 where buf[len] == 0
FUNCTION sds_create(initial_bytes):
    n = length(initial_bytes)
    alloc = compute_new_capacity(n)
    buf = byte_array_of_length(alloc + 1)
    copy_bytes(initial_bytes, 0, buf, 0, n)
    buf[n] = 0
    RETURN SDS(len = n, alloc = alloc, buf = buf)
    new_buf = byte_array_of_length(s.alloc + 1)
    copy_bytes(s.buf, 0, new_buf, 0, s.len)
    new_buf[s.len] = 0
    RETURN SDS(len = s.len, alloc = s.alloc, buf = new_buf)
    free_memory(s.buf)    RETURN s.len    RETURN s.alloc - s.len
FUNCTION compute_new_capacity(required):
    IF required < 1:    IF required < SDS_MAX_PREALLOC:        WHILE cap < required:        RETURN cap
    RETURN required + SDS_MAX_PREALLOC
FUNCTION sds_make_room_for(s, addlen):
    avail = s.alloc - s.len        RETURN s    newcap = compute_new_capacity(newlen)
    new_buf = byte_array_of_length(newcap + 1)
    copy_bytes(s.buf, 0, new_buf, 0, s.len)    free_memory(s.buf)    s.alloc = newcap
FUNCTION sds_catlen(s, data, datalen):
    s = sds_make_room_for(s, datalen)
    copy_bytes(data, 0, s.buf, s.len, datalen)
    s.len = s.len + datalen    RETURN s
FUNCTION sds_cat(s, str):
    RETURN sds_catlen(s, str, length(str))
FUNCTION sds_cat_char(s, c):
    s = sds_make_room_for(s, 1)    s.len = s.len + 1    RETURN s
FUNCTION sds_copylen(s, data, datalen):
    IF datalen <= s.alloc:
        copy_bytes(data, 0, s.buf, 0, datalen)
        s.len = datalen        RETURN s
    newcap = compute_new_capacity(datalen)
    new_buf = byte_array_of_length(newcap + 1)
    copy_bytes(data, 0, new_buf, 0, datalen)
    new_buf[datalen] = 0    s.buf = new_buf    s.alloc = newcap    s.len = 0        s.buf[0] = 0
FUNCTION sds_trim(s, cset):
    start = 0    WHILE start <= end AND byte_in_set(s.buf[start], cset):    WHILE end >= start AND byte_in_set(s.buf[end], cset):    newlen = (start <= end) ? (end - start + 1) : 0
    IF start > 0 AND newlen > 0:
        move_bytes(s.buf, start, s.buf, 0, newlen)    s.buf[s.len] = 0
FUNCTION sds_range(s, start, end):
    IF s.len == 0:    IF start < 0:    IF end < 0:    IF start < 0:    IF end >= s.len:    IF start > end:        s.buf[0] = 0    newlen = end - start + 1        move_bytes(s.buf, start, s.buf, 0, newlen)    s.buf[s.len] = 0
FUNCTION sds_delete_range(s, pos, lenr):
    IF lenr <= 0 OR s.len == 0:    IF pos < 0:    IF pos < 0:    IF pos >= s.len:    tail = pos + lenr        s.len = pos        RETURN s
    move_bytes(s.buf, tail, s.buf, pos, s.len - tail)
    s.len = s.len - (tail - pos)    RETURN s
FUNCTION sds_insert(s, pos, data, datalen):
    IF pos < 0:    IF pos < 0:    IF pos > s.len:    s = sds_make_room_for(s, datalen)
    move_bytes(s.buf, pos, s.buf, pos + datalen, s.len - pos)
    copy_bytes(data, 0, s.buf, pos, datalen)    s.buf[s.len] = 0    minlen = (s.len < t.len) ? s.len : t.len
    r = memcmp(s.buf, t.buf, minlen)
    IF r == 0:            RETURN 0
        RETURN (s.len < t.len) ? -1 : 1
    RETURN (r < 0) ? -1 : 1
FUNCTION sds_resize(s, newlen):
    IF newlen <= s.alloc:            s.len = newlen        ELSE:            s.buf[s.len] = 0    newcap = compute_new_capacity(newlen)
    new_buf = byte_array_of_length(newcap + 1)
    copy_bytes(s.buf, 0, new_buf, 0, s.len)    free_memory(s.buf)    s.alloc = newcap    s.buf[s.len] = 0

Execution flow (operational sequence)

  1. Create: sds_create allocates a buffer sized by compute_new_capacity and stores the initial bytes; len and alloc are set and buffer is NUL-terminated.
  2. Read: sds_len returns length in O(1); sds_avail returns free space in O(1).
  3. Append: sds_cat/sds_catlen call sds_make_room_for which grows the buffer if needed according to compute_new_capacity, then copies payload and updates len and NUL terminator.
  4. Insert/delete/trim/range: in-place memory moves adjust payload and update len and terminator; operations that expand ensure room first.
  5. Resize/grow: compute_new_capacity uses exponential doubling up to SDS_MAX_PREALLOC and then linear growth by adding SDS_MAX_PREALLOC, which bounds realloc frequency for small and medium growth and avoids excessive preallocation for very large strings.
  6. Duplicate/free: sds_dup produces an independent SDS; sds_free releases memory.

System-level rationale and trade-offs

  • Storing len in the header eliminates O(n) length queries and simplifies safe binary-string handling.
  • The growth policy (doubling below a threshold, then linear bump) reduces the number of reallocations for common append patterns while controlling worst-case memory headroom for enormous strings.
  • Binary-safety and a trailing NUL ensure interoperability with C APIs and existing code while preserving embedded zero bytes.
  • The simple header and contiguous buffer keep per-string overhead minimal and provide excellent cache locality.
  • Most operations are implemented with a single realloc when capacity must increase; mutation-heavy workloads can pay realloc costs for very large strings, but the amortized policy keeps these reasonable.
  • The API is deliberately small and deterministic to fit Redis’ single-threaded, performance-sensitive environment and to make memory accounting predictable.

The true significance of these algorithms emerges only when they are viewed as a coordinated system rather than as individual optimizations. The hash table provides fast access, but incremental rehashing ensures that this speed does not collapse under growth. Open addressing reduces memory indirection, reinforcing cache efficiency and predictability. Compact encodings such as ziplist, listpack, and intset exploit common data patterns to minimize memory footprint without sacrificing correctness. Quicklist balances mutability and locality, and SDS establishes a safe, performant string foundation that everything else depends on.

What unifies these designs is a consistent philosophy: trade rare, catastrophic costs for frequent, bounded ones; favor memory locality over abstraction purity; and adapt representation dynamically as data evolves. These algorithms are explicitly engineered to operate within tight operational constraints — single-threaded execution, strict latency budgets, and unpredictable workloads — while still scaling to millions of keys and operations per second.

In practical terms, this architecture demonstrates that high performance is not achieved through a single “fast” data structure, but through careful composition and transition strategies between multiple representations. Each algorithm plays a specific role in controlling time complexity, memory layout, and runtime behavior. The result is a system that remains responsive, efficient, and robust even as workload characteristics shift.

Ultimately, these algorithms illustrate a critical systems-engineering lesson: performance at scale is a property of the whole, not the parts. Mastery lies in understanding not only how each structure works, but why and when the system chooses one representation over another — and how those choices preserve correctness, efficiency, and predictability under real-world conditions.

items.title: The “Bus Factor” Is Real: Why Undocumented Code Is a Ticking Time Bomb
items.author: Hui Zhu
items.content:
There is a specific kind of horror reserved for developers. It isn’t a server outage. It isn’t a data breach. It is opening a file you wrote six months ago and having absolutely no idea what it does.

We tell ourselves lies to sleep better at night. “The code is self-documenting,” we say. “I’ll write the JSDoc later,” we promise. But “later” never comes, and that brilliant algorithm you wrote becomes a black box that everyone is terrified to touch.

This is how “Legacy Code” is born. Not by age, but by silence.

Documentation is the only thing that separates a maintainable asset from a technical liability. But let’s be real: writing documentation is tedious. It breaks your flow. It feels like administrative work when you just want to build features.

What if you could outsource the “boring” part of documentation while keeping the “thinking” part?

I created the Code Documentation Specialist prompt to solve exactly this problem. It doesn’t just parse your code; it interviews it. It forces the “why” out of the “how.”

The “Rosetta Stone” Prompt

Most AI documentation tools just describe what the code is doing line-by-line. That is useless. You can read the code to see it does. You need to know it exists, to use it, and breaks it.

This prompt acts as a meticulous Technical Writer who refuses to accept ambiguity. It generates documentation that is:

  1. : It explains the purpose, not just the syntax.
  2. : It highlights error handling and edge cases.
  3. : It provides copy-paste usage examples.

Copy this instruction into your AI assistant (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) to turn your cryptic functions into clear contracts.

# Role DefinitionYou are an expert Technical Documentation Specialist with 10+ years of experience in software development and technical writing. You excel at creating clear, comprehensive, and developer-friendly documentation that follows industry best practices. Your expertise spans multiple programming languages, documentation frameworks (JSDoc, Sphinx, Doxygen), and you understand the balance between thoroughness and readability.
# Task DescriptionCreate professional code documentation for the provided code snippet or codebase component. Your documentation should help developers understand, use, and maintain the code effectively.
Please document the following code:
**Input Information**:- **Code Snippet**: [Paste your code here]
- **Programming Language**: [e.g., Python, JavaScript, Java, etc.]
- **Documentation Style**: [e.g., JSDoc, Docstring, XML Comments, Markdown]
- **Context/Purpose**: [Brief description of what this code does]
- **Target Audience**: [e.g., Junior developers, API consumers, Internal team]
## 1. Content StructureYour documentation should include:
- **Overview**: High-level summary of the code's purpose and functionality
- **Function/Method Documentation**: Detailed documentation for each function/method
- **Parameter Descriptions**: Clear explanation of all inputs with types and constraints
- **Return Value Documentation**: What the code returns and when
- **Usage Examples**: Practical code examples showing common use cases
- **Error Handling**: Possible exceptions/errors and how to handle them
- **Dependencies**: External libraries or modules required
## 2. Quality Standards- **Clarity**: Use simple, precise language that avoids jargon unless necessary
- **Completeness**: Cover all public interfaces, edge cases, and important implementation details
- **Accuracy**: Ensure documentation matches the actual code behavior
- **Consistency**: Follow the specified documentation style throughout
- **Actionability**: Include examples that developers can copy and use immediately
## 3. Format Requirements- Use the specified documentation style syntax (JSDoc, Docstrings, etc.)
- Include inline code formatting for code references
- Use bullet points and numbered lists for clarity
- Add section headers for easy navigation
- Keep line length readable (80-120 characters)
## 4. Style Constraints- **Language Style**: Technical but accessible; avoid unnecessary complexity
- **Tone**: Professional, helpful, and encouraging
- **Perspective**: Second person ("you") for instructions, third person for descriptions
- **Technical Level**: Match the specified target audience
Before completing your output, verify:- [ ] All public functions/methods are documented
- [ ] Parameter types and descriptions are complete
- [ ] Return values are clearly explained
- [ ] At least one usage example is provided
- [ ] Error scenarios are documented
- [ ] Documentation follows the specified style guide
- [ ] No placeholder text remains
- [ ] Code examples are syntactically correct
# Important Notes- Do NOT modify the original code unless specifically requested
- Preserve existing documentation and enhance it
- Flag any potential issues or ambiguities in the code
- Suggest documentation improvements for code maintainability
# Output FormatProvide the documentation in a format ready to be inserted into the codebase:
1. Inline documentation (above functions/classes)
2. A separate README section if applicable
3. Any additional notes or recommendations

Why This Prompt Actually Works

You might think, “Can’t I just ask ChatGPT to ‘add comments’?” You can, but you’ll get garbage. You’ll get comments like // increments i by 1 above a line that says i++.

This prompt uses a few specific psychological triggers to get better results:

1. The “Usage Example” Mandate

Notice the requirement for . This is the single most valuable part of any documentation. By forcing the AI to generate a working example, you implicitly verify that the AI understands how the code is to be used. If the AI can’t write a valid example, it means your code might be too complex.

2. The “Error Handling” Focus

Most developers document the “Happy Path” — what happens when everything goes right. This prompt explicitly demands an section. It forces the documentation to answer: “What happens if I pass null?" or "What if the API is down?" This is the information that saves you during a 3 AM debugging session.

3. The “Target Audience” Parameter

Documentation for a Senior Architect should look different than documentation for a Junior Intern. By specifying the , you tune the AI’s output. It avoids over-explaining basic concepts to experts, or using obscure jargon with beginners.

How to Apply It (Without Going Crazy)

You don’t need to document your entire codebase overnight. That is a recipe for burnout. instead, try “Just-In-Time” Documentation:

  • Before you touch that messy function, run it through this prompt to understand what it currently does.
  • When a new team member asks “What does this class do?”, generate the docs and commit them.
  • Make it a rule. No PR gets merged without documentation generated by this standard.

Your code is a story. Make sure it’s one that others — and your future self — can actually read.

items.title: Kubernetes 1.35: Enhanced Debugging with Versioned z-pages APIs
items.content:
Debugging Kubernetes control plane components can be challenging, especially when you need to quickly understand the runtime state of a component or verify its configuration. With Kubernetes 1.35, we're enhancing the z-pages debugging endpoints with structured, machine-parseable responses that make it easier to build tooling and automate troubleshooting workflows.

z-pages are special debugging endpoints exposed by Kubernetes control plane components. Introduced as an alpha feature in Kubernetes 1.32, these endpoints provide runtime diagnostics for components like , , , and . The name "z-pages" comes from the convention of using paths for debugging endpoints.

Currently, Kubernetes supports two primary z-page endpoints:

Displays high-level component information including version information, start time, uptime, and available debug paths
Shows all command-line arguments and their values used to start the component (with confidential values redacted for security)

These endpoints are valuable for human operators who need to quickly inspect component state, but until now, they only returned plain text output that was difficult to parse programmatically.

What's new in Kubernetes 1.35?

Kubernetes 1.35 introduces structured, versioned responses for both and endpoints. This enhancement maintains backward compatibility with the existing plain text format while adding support for machine-readable JSON responses.

The new structured responses are opt-in. Without specifying an header, the endpoints continue to return the familiar plain text format:

$ curl --cert /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-kubelet-client.crt \
--key /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-kubelet-client.key \
--cacert /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
https://localhost:6443/statusz
kube-apiserver statusz
Warning: This endpoint is not meant to be machine parseable, has no formatting compatibility guarantees and is for debugging purposes only.
Started: Wed Oct 16 21:03:43 UTC 2024
Up: 0 hr 00 min 16 sec
Go version: go1.23.2
Binary version: 1.35.0-alpha.0.1595
Emulation version: 1.35
Paths: /healthz /livez /metrics /readyz /statusz /version

Structured JSON responses

To receive a structured response, include the appropriate header:

Accept: application/json;v=v1alpha1;g=config.k8s.io;as=Statusz

This returns a versioned JSON response:

Similarly, supports structured responses with the header:

Accept: application/json;v=v1alpha1;g=config.k8s.io;as=Flagz

Why structured responses matter

The addition of structured responses opens up several new possibilities:

1. Automated health checks and monitoring

Instead of parsing plain text, monitoring tools can now easily extract specific fields. For example, you can programmatically check if a component has been running with an unexpected emulated version or verify that critical flags are set correctly.

Developers can build sophisticated debugging tools that compare configurations across multiple components or track configuration drift over time. The structured format makes it trivial to configurations or validate that components are running with expected settings.

3. API versioning and stability

By introducing versioned APIs (starting with ), we provide a clear path to stability. As the feature matures, we'll introduce and eventually , giving you confidence that your tooling won't break with future Kubernetes releases.

How to use structured z-pages

Both endpoints require feature gates to be enabled:

  • : Enable the feature gate
  • : Enable the feature gate

Example: Getting structured responses

Here's an example using to retrieve structured JSON responses from the kube-apiserver:

The examples above use client certificate authentication and verify the server's certificate using . If you need to bypass certificate verification in a test environment, you can use (or ), but this should never be done in production as it makes you vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks.

The structured z-page responses are an feature in Kubernetes 1.35. This means:

  • The API format may change in future releases
  • These endpoints are intended for debugging, not production automation
  • You should avoid relying on them for critical monitoring workflows until they reach beta or stable status

Security and access control

z-pages expose internal component information and require proper access controls. Here are the key security considerations:

: Access to z-page endpoints is restricted to members of the group, which follows the same authorization model as other debugging endpoints like , , and . This ensures that only authorized users and service accounts can access debugging information. If your cluster uses RBAC, you can manage access by granting appropriate permissions to this group.

: The authentication requirements for these endpoints depend on your cluster's configuration. Unless anonymous authentication is enabled for your cluster, you typically need to use authentication mechanisms (such as client certificates) to access these endpoints.

: These endpoints reveal configuration details about your cluster components, including:

  • Component versions and build information
  • All command-line arguments and their values (with confidential values redacted)
  • Available debug endpoints

Only grant access to trusted operators and debugging tools. Avoid exposing these endpoints to unauthorized users or automated systems that don't require this level of access.

As the feature matures, we (Kubernetes SIG Instrumentation) expect to:

  • Introduce and eventually versions of the API
  • Gather community feedback on the response schema
  • Potentially add additional z-page endpoints based on user needs

We encourage you to experiment with structured z-pages in a test environment:

  1. Enable the and feature gates on your control plane components
  2. Try querying the endpoints with both plain text and structured formats
  3. Build a simple tool or script that uses the structured data
  4. Share your feedback with the community

We'd love to hear your feedback! The structured z-pages feature is designed to make Kubernetes easier to debug and monitor. Whether you're building internal tooling, contributing to open source projects, or just exploring the feature, your input helps shape the future of Kubernetes observability.

If you have questions, suggestions, or run into issues, please reach out to SIG Instrumentation. You can find us on Slack or at our regular community meetings.
