items.title: We Thought Docker Was Secure. Then We Read the Audit Report
items.author: Devrim Ozcay
items.content:
The container security assumptions that almost destroyed our startup

Third-party security audit results came back on a Tuesday.

I was eating lunch, scrolling through Slack, when our CTO dropped the PDF in the engineering channel.

“Read this. We have a problem.”

138-page security audit. Executive summary on page 2. I skipped straight there like everyone does.

Critical vulnerabilities: 7

We’d dockerized everything six months ago. Felt so modern. So secure. Containers are isolated, right? That’s literally the whole point.

Turns out, we didn’t understand containers at all.

The Docker Migration That Felt So Right

Let me rewind to January 2024.

We were running a Spring Boot monolith directly on EC2. Deployment meant SSH-ing into servers, stopping the process, replacing the JAR, restarting. Sometimes it worked. Sometimes port 8080 was already in use and we had to kill phantom processes.

Our new DevOps hire (let’s call him Jake) proposed containerization.

“Docker will solve everything,” he said. “Consistent environments. Easy scaling. No more ‘works on my machine’ problems.”

He wasn’t wrong about those benefits. But he also wasn’t right about security.

We dockerized our Spring Boot apps, our Python data processors, our Node.js frontend builds. Everything went into containers. Kubernetes orchestrated it all. We felt like real engineers.

Deployment went from 45 minutes of manual SSH hell to 5 minutes of kubectl apply. Beautiful.

The Assumptions We Made (All Wrong)

Here’s what we thought about Docker security:

Assumption 1: Containers are isolated, so they’re secure

Reality: Container isolation is not VM-level isolation. It’s process-level isolation. Big difference.

Our containers were running as root. All of them. Because that’s the default and we never questioned it.

The audit report had a whole section on this. With screenshots. Of our containers. Running privileged mode. With access to the host’s Docker socket.

Translation: If someone compromised any of our containers, they had root access to the entire host. And potentially every other container on that host.

We basically built a security theater. Looked secure. Wasn’t.

Assumption 2: Official images from Docker Hub are safe

Reality: “Official” doesn’t mean what we thought it meant.

We were using openjdk:11 as our base image. Official Java image. Thousands of pulls. Must be trustworthy, right?

The audit flagged it immediately.

317 vulnerabilities. Including 47 high-severity CVEs.

The image hadn’t been updated in 18 months. Java 11 itself was fine, but the underlying OS packages (Debian) were ancient. Unpatched. Full of known exploits.

We’d been shipping vulnerable containers to production for six months.

Assumption 3: If we’re not exposing ports publicly, we’re safe

Reality: Network isolation in Docker is more complex than we understood.

Our containers were in the same Docker network. They could talk to each other freely. Which sounds fine until you realize what that actually means.

If someone compromised our frontend container (Node.js, lots of npm packages, high attack surface), they could pivot to our backend containers. Which had database credentials. As environment variables. In plaintext.

The audit found our database password in three different places:

  • Docker Compose file (committed to Git)
  • Environment variables in running containers
  • Container logs (because Spring Boot helpfully prints startup config)

Assumption 4: Scanning images in CI/CD is enough

We’d set up Trivy to scan our images during builds. Felt responsible. Proactive even.

The problem? We ignored the results.

Trivy would flag vulnerabilities. Our CI pipeline would… proceed anyway. We had no policy for what to do with the findings. No process for patching. No ownership.

The audit pointed this out, diplomatically: “Container scanning is implemented but findings are not remediated.”

Translation: You’re collecting data and doing nothing with it.

The Seven Critical Vulnerabilities That Kept Me Up

Let me break down what the audit actually found. Not the abstract stuff. The specific “oh shit” moments.

Critical #1: Containers Running as Root

Every single container. Root user. Full privileges.

Why? Because nobody told us otherwise and Docker defaults to root.

The fix was embarrassingly simple:

# Before (default, vulnerable)FROM openjdk:11ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "/app.jar"]FROM openjdk:11
RUN groupadd -r appuser && useradd -r -g appuser appuser
USER appuserENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "/app.jar"]

Three lines. That’s all it took to not run as root.

We’d been vulnerable for six months because we didn’t add three lines.

Critical #2: Exposed Docker Socket

Our logging container needed access to Docker to collect logs from other containers.

Jake’s solution? Mount the Docker socket.

volumes:  - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock

Seems practical. Very convenient. Also catastrophically insecure.

That socket is basically root access to the entire Docker daemon. Anyone who compromises that container can:

  • Access volumes from other containers
  • Read environment variables from everything
  • Basically own the entire host

The audit report had a note here that just said: “This is equivalent to giving the container root access to the host. Do not do this.”

We removed that mount immediately and switched to a proper logging driver. Should’ve done that from day one.

Critical #3: Secrets in Environment Variables

Our Spring Boot database config looked like this:

environment:  - SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL=jdbc:postgresql://db:5432/prod
  - SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME=admin
  - SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD=SuperSecret123!

Clean. Simple. Completely insecure.

Environment variables are visible to anyone with access to the container. Or the host. Or the orchestrator. Or the logs.

docker inspect container_name shows all environment variables. No authentication needed if you have Docker access.

We switched to Docker secrets for sensitive data. Better late than never.

Critical #4: Outdated Base Images

Remember that openjdk:11 image with 317 vulnerabilities?

We thought “official image” meant “maintained image.” Nope.

Many official images are community-maintained. They get abandoned. They don’t get patched. But they stay “official” on Docker Hub because nobody removes them.

We switched to actively maintained images. eclipse-temurin:11-jre for Java. python:3.11-slim for Python. And we started actually updating them.

Set up Renovate bot to create PRs when base images update. Now we at least know when we’re vulnerable.

Critical #5: Unrestricted Network Access Between Containers

All our containers could talk to all other containers. No firewall rules. No network policies. Just a flat network where everything trusts everything.

The audit simulated a compromise of our frontend container. From there, they:

  • Connected directly to PostgreSQL (port 5432 was accessible)
  • Accessed Redis (port 6379, no auth)
  • Reached our internal admin API (port 8081, no authentication)

We implemented Kubernetes NetworkPolicies to restrict traffic. Frontend can only talk to backend. Backend can only talk to database. Nothing else.

Should’ve been like that from the start.

Critical #6: No Resource Limits

Our containers had no CPU or memory limits.

One bad Python script with a memory leak could OOM-kill the entire node, taking down every other container with it.

Which happened. Twice. Before we read the audit report.

Adding limits was trivial:

resources:  limits:    cpu: "500m"    memory: "256Mi"

Now misbehaving containers just crash themselves instead of taking down neighbors.

Critical #7: Privileged Containers

One of our containers needed to modify network interfaces. Jake’s solution?

That flag removes all container isolation. It’s basically “run this container as if it’s not a container.”

Privileged containers can:

  • Access all devices on the host
  • Bypass all security restrictions

There’s almost never a good reason to use privileged mode. If you think you need it, you probably need to rethink your architecture.

We refactored that service to not need host network access. Problem solved.

The Remediation Sprint From Hell

After reading the audit, we had two weeks to fix everything before the board meeting where we had to present our security posture.

Two weeks to unfuck six months of bad decisions.

Week 1: Stop the Bleeding

Day 1–2: Removed all privileged containers and Docker socket mounts Day 3–4: Switched to non-root users in all Dockerfiles

Day 5: Moved secrets from environment variables to Docker secrets

Quick wins. Low-hanging fruit. The obvious stuff we should’ve done initially.

Day 6–8: Updated all base images, fixed vulnerabilities Day 9–10: Implemented network policies

Day 11–12: Set up proper image scanning with enforceable policies

Day 13–14: Documentation, training, postmortem

We made the deadline. Barely.

The board meeting went… fine. We presented our findings honestly, showed what we’d fixed, and outlined ongoing improvements.

Nobody got fired. But trust was damaged. The “move fast and break things” mentality suddenly felt less cute.

What We Actually Learned About Container Security

Docker is not inherently secure. It’s a tool. You can use it securely or insecurely.

We chose insecure because we didn’t know better.

Here’s what actually matters for container security:

Lesson 1: Least Privilege Is Not Optional

Run as non-root. Always. No exceptions.

Need specific capabilities? Grant only those capabilities, not full root.

FROM python:3.11-slimRUN useradd -m -u 1000 appuserWORKDIR /app
COPY --chown=appuser:appuser . .
CMD ["python", "app.py"]

This pattern works for everything. Java, Python, Node, whatever.

Lesson 2: Base Images Matter More Than You Think

Don’t just grab the first image that works. Actually evaluate:

  • When was it last updated?
  • How many vulnerabilities does it have?
  • Is there a slimmer, more maintained alternative?

We switched to -slim variants wherever possible. Smaller images, fewer dependencies, smaller attack surface.

For Java specifically, we moved from openjdk (abandoned) to eclipse-temurin (actively maintained by Eclipse Foundation).

Lesson 3: Secrets Management Is Hard, Do It Right

  • Environment variables (directly)

Use a proper secrets management system. Docker Secrets. Kubernetes Secrets with encryption at rest. HashiCorp Vault. AWS Secrets Manager. Whatever.

Just not environment variables in plaintext.

Lesson 4: Network Isolation Isn’t Automatic

Default Docker networking is convenient and insecure.

Implement network policies. Limit what can talk to what. Defense in depth.

Your frontend doesn’t need direct database access. Your data processing containers don’t need to reach external APIs. Enforce this at the network level.

Lesson 5: Scanning Is Useless Without Action

We were scanning images. We just weren’t doing anything with the results.

  • Critical vulnerabilities? Build fails.
  • High severity? Requires manual approval and documented acceptance of risk.
  • Medium/Low? Fix within 30 days.

Make someone responsible for vulnerability remediation. Otherwise it never happens.

Lesson 6: Containers Are Processes, Not VMs

This was our fundamental misunderstanding.

Containers share the host kernel. They’re isolated processes, not separate machines. That isolation can be bypassed if you’re not careful.

Don’t assume container boundaries are security boundaries without proper configuration.

The Real Cost of Security Ignorance

The audit cost us $15,000. Fair price for a thorough security review.

The remediation sprint cost us two weeks of engineering time. Four engineers, full time. Roughly $40,000 in salary costs.

The opportunity cost? We delayed two major features. Lost competitive advantage. Disappointed customers waiting for promised functionality.

Total cost of not understanding container security: ~$55,000 plus intangibles.

The three Docker security lines we should’ve written six months ago would’ve cost us… 10 minutes?

The math is depressing when you write it out like that.

After this experience, we now use:

for vulnerability scanning (and actually act on findings)Docker Bench for Security to audit Docker host configuration for runtime security monitoringKubernetes Pod Security Standards to enforce baseline security as a second opinion on vulnerabilities

And we actually read the documentation now instead of copying Stack Overflow snippets blindly.

For folks building with Java/Spring (like we are), I keep a few practical resources nearby that helped me understand production security better:

Grokking the Spring Boot Interview covers security concepts like proper configuration, transaction boundaries, and common vulnerabilities in a way that actually makes sense.

If you’re dealing with production systems in general (any language), the Production Engineering Cheatsheet covers the fundamentals nobody teaches until things break — including container security basics.

The Uncomfortable Truth Nobody Tells You

Most companies are running insecure containers right now.

They just haven’t been audited yet.

The defaults are wrong. The tutorials are incomplete. The “getting started” guides prioritize convenience over security.

You can learn this the easy way (reading about others’ mistakes) or the hard way (audit report with 7 critical vulnerabilities).

We chose the hard way. You don’t have to.

What We Changed After the Audit

Security became part of definition of done. Features aren’t “complete” until they pass security review.

We hired a security engineer. Expensive, but cheaper than the next audit finding major issues.

We implemented monthly security training. Not boring compliance theater. Real stuff. “Here’s how we got pwned, don’t make the same mistake.”

We document security decisions. Why we chose this image. Why we need this capability. Why we accepted this risk.

And we stopped assuming anything is secure by default.

Trust, but verify. Then verify again.

The Resources That Actually Helped

If you’re serious about not repeating our mistakes, here’s what helped us level up:

For debugging production issues when security configurations break things (and they will), I built a small free tool that helps with latency analysis and common misconfigurations:

For test data generation without using production dumps (GDPR nightmare), we built:

And if you’re building production backends with Spring Boot specifically:

I’m not selling motivation — just tools built from painful experience.

What You Should Do Tomorrow

If you’re running Docker in production:

Run docker scan on your images. Right now. See what's actually in there.

Check if your containers run as root: docker inspect container_name | grep User

Look for privileged containers: docker inspect container_name | grep Privileged

Audit your secrets. Are any in environment variables? Git repos? Container images?

Review your network policies. Can everything talk to everything?

If any of these checks fail (they probably will), you have work to do.

Better to find out now than via audit report.

Security isn’t a feature you add later. It’s a foundation you build on.

We treated containerization as a deployment optimization. Better DevEx. Faster builds. Easier scaling.

We didn’t treat it as a security-critical infrastructure change requiring proper planning and review.

Containers are great. Docker is powerful. Kubernetes is useful.

But none of them are secure by default. Security is something you configure, implement, and maintain. Actively.

The defaults will let you shoot yourself in the foot. It’s your job to not pull the trigger.

Check your containers right now. Run docker ps and pick any container. Is it running as root? Does it have unnecessary privileges?

Drop what you find in the comments. Let’s learn from each other’s mistakes before they become audit findings.

And if you’re about to containerize your app, learn from our pain. Read the security docs BEFORE deploying, not AFTER the audit.

Your future self will thank you. And your security team won’t hate you.

Now if you’ll excuse me, I have to go update 47 base images that Renovate flagged this morning.

The work never ends. But at least now we’re doing it right.

items.title: Running Self-Managed GitLab Runners on Kubernetes — What Works, What Breaks, and Why
items.author: Oleh Adam Dubnytskyy
items.content:
Beyond Setup — Running Runners in Production

Running Self-Managed GitLab Runners inside a Kubernetes cluster is a natural step once CI workloads start to grow. On paper, it offers a clean separation of concerns: GitLab handles orchestration, Kubernetes handles execution, and autoscaling promises efficient resource usage.

In practice, this setup works well — until the system is exposed to real load, upgrades, and failures. Most issues don’t show up during the initial rollout. They appear weeks or months later, usually during a routine maintenance window or a spike in pipeline activity.

This section focuses on those .

Insights From Running GitLab Runners in Production

The Runner Manager Pod Is a Single Point of Fragility

Kubernetes provides strong guarantees around pod scheduling and restarts, which can create a false sense of safety. While build pods are ephemeral by design, the GitLab Runner manager pod plays a critical coordinating role: it polls GitLab for jobs, spawns build pods, and streams logs back to the GitLab API.

Under normal conditions, this works without issue. The problem appears during:

    If the manager pod is terminated while jobs are running, those jobs can fail or lose log output. Even though the build pods may still be running, the coordination layer is gone.

    This behavior is subtle because it doesn’t violate Kubernetes semantics — it violates expectations about job continuity.

    Mitigations that helped in practice:

    • Configure cleanup_grace_period_seconds. When a job completes, the duration in seconds that the pod has to terminate gracefully. After this period, the processes are forcibly halted with a kill signal. Ignored if terminationGracePeriodSeconds is specified
    • Configure pod_termination_grace_period_seconds. Pod-level setting which determines the duration in seconds which the pod has to terminate gracefully. After this, the processes are forcibly halted with a kill signal. Ignored if terminationGracePeriodSeconds is specified.
    • Avoiding aggressive rolling updates of the runner deployment
    • Treating runner upgrades as , not routine redeployments

    Scheduling Failures Are Usually Resource Problems

    The Kubernetes executor waits for build pods to be scheduled. If the cluster cannot satisfy the requested resources, jobs fail with timeouts.

    This commonly happens when:

    • CPU or memory requests are set optimistically
    • node autoscaling lags behind CI demand
    • multiple pipelines start simultaneously

    The failure mode is misleading. The runner reports a timeout, but the real issue is cluster capacity.

    • node group instance types
    • configure the maximum number of jobs. Limits how many jobs can run concurrently, across all registered runners.

    is more important than tuning executor timeouts.

    CI workloads are bursty by nature. The cluster must be designed for that reality.

    Setup and Prerequisites. This section outlines how the runners were deployed.
    1. Kubernetes cluster. In our scenario, we will provision Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service cluster with help of robust, production-ready community module called terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws made by Bryant Biggs.

    2. GitLab server. In our scenario, GitLab server runs on Amazon EC2 instance.

    Let’s layout the whole picture by drawing architecture.

    Let’s provision EKS cluster in AWS cloud. Baseline configuration consists of specifying: cluster version, cluster name, VPC, VPC Subnets,

    1. coredns, kube-proxy, vpc-cni add-ons
    2. Minimalistic EKS Managed Node Group with 2 nodes

    To setup GitLab Runners in Kubernetes environment we will leverage terraform hashicorp/kubernetes and hashicorp/helm providers.

    With that said, here goes GitLab pipeline example:

    Running GitLab Runners in Kubernetes is not inherently complex, but it is at first glance. Most issues don’t come from misconfiguration — they come from assumptions that hold during initial testing but break under real conditions.

    To push the boundaries of CI infrastructure even further, we will introduce Karpenter — an Open-Source High-Performance Kubernetes Cluster Autoscaler in upcoming post. Stay tuned!

Day 5 of the KodeKloud Cloud Engineer (AWS) Challenge is completed ✅ The challenge is getting more practical and closer to real-world cloud engineering every day.

In simple terms, a is where your data is stored.

Compute without storage is useless.

🧠 Real-World Scenario (Why Storage Matters)

Imagine you’re running a .

One day, your team complains:

    You log in, check disk usage, and realize: 👉 The disk is almost full.

    • Extend the volume using 

    In AWS, this same problem is solved using .

    Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS) provides persistent block-level storage for EC2 instances.

    • Storage can be
    • Data persists even if the instance stops
    • Designed for performance, scalability, and reliability

    EBS is a for almost every AWS workload.

    📊 AWS EBS Volume Types (All 6) — When to Use What

    AWS provides , each optimized for a specific workload. Choosing the wrong one wastes money or kills performance.

    🔹 1. General Purpose SSD (gp3) — Default Choice

    • Small to medium databases

    ✅ Use this unless you have a strong reason not to

    🔹 2. General Purpose SSD (gp2) — Legacy

    Existing workloads only

    • IOPS scales with volume size

    ❌ Not recommended for new workloads AWS recommends .

    🔹 3. Provisioned IOPS SSD (io1 / io2) — High Performance

    Mission-critical databases

      ⚠️ Use only when performance SLAs demand it.

      🔹 4. Throughput Optimized HDD (st1)

      Large sequential workloads

      • Optimized for throughput, not IOPS

      ❌ Not suitable for boot volumes ❌ Not for random I/O

      Infrequently accessed data

          ⚠️ Use only when cost matters more than speed.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.35: Watch Based Route Reconciliation in the Cloud Controller Manager
items.content:
Up to and including Kubernetes v1.34, the route controller in Cloud Controller Manager (CCM) implementations built using the k8s.io/cloud-provider library reconciles routes at a fixed interval. This causes unnecessary API requests to the cloud provider when there are no changes to routes. Other controllers implemented through the same library already use watch-based mechanisms, leveraging informers to avoid unnecessary API calls. A new feature gate is being introduced in v1.35 to allow changing the behavior of the route controller to use watch-based informers.

The feature gate CloudControllerManagerWatchBasedRoutesReconciliation has been introduced to k8s.io/cloud-provider in alpha stage by SIG Cloud Provider. To enable this feature you can use --feature-gate=CloudControllerManagerWatchBasedRoutesReconciliation=true in the CCM implementation you are using.

This feature gate will trigger the route reconciliation loop whenever a node is added, deleted, or the fields or are updated.

An additional reconcile is performed in a random interval between 12h and 24h, which is chosen at the controller's start time.

This feature gate does not modify the logic within the reconciliation loop. Therefore, users of a CCM implementation should not experience significant changes to their existing route configurations.

items.title: Securing US Health Data on Google Cloud
items.author: Mohamed Rasvi
items.content:
In the realm of digital healthcare, trust is currency. When you are entrusted with Health Protected Information (HPI), “secure enough” doesn’t cut it. You need robust, verifiable proof that patient data stays where it’s supposed to stay and is only seen by who is supposed to see it.

The challenge comes when moving to the public cloud. How do you ensure data sovereignty (keeping data in the US) while preventing modern data exfiltration threats?

The “Why”: The Risks We Are Mitigating

We aren’t just trying to stop hackers; we are trying to prevent misconfiguration and insider threats. We need to solve for:

  • A well-meaning developer accidentally spinning up a VM in the Frankfurt region to test latency, violating US-only requirements.
  • An administrator having permission to manage a database, but nobody knowing if they actually specific patient records.
  • A user with legitimate access to HPI data copying it to their personal Google Drive bucket to work on it over the weekend.

Layer 1: The Foundation: Assured Workloads

Many assume compliance is applied project-by-project. In reality, it starts at the Organization level.

Google Cloud Assured Workloads allows you to create a secure boundary manifested as a in your resource hierarchy that pre-configures compliance requirements.

When we create an Assured Workload using the “US Data Boundary for Healthcare and Life Sciences” regime, GCP automatically does two critical things:

  • It creates a Folder that acts as a container for your regulated projects.
  • It applies a rigid on that folder that denies the creation of resources (like storage buckets or compute instances) outside of US regions.

If a developer tries to create a resource in asia-northeast1 inside this folder, the API simply says "No."

Layer 2: Radical Transparency, Data Access Logging

By default, Google Cloud logs “Admin Activity” (who changed the configuration). For health data, this is insufficient. We need to know who accessed the .

We must enable at the Assured Workload Folder level.

By turning on DATA_READ and DATA_WRITE logging for services like BigQuery and Cloud Storage, we generate an immutable audit trail. Every time a SELECT * query is run against patient data, or a file is downloaded from storage, a log entry is generated containing the user's identity, IP address, and the exact resource accessed.

Layer 3: The Digital Air Gap, VPC Service Controls

This is the most critical layer for preventing data exfiltration.

Assured Workloads keeps your data resident in the US, but it doesn’t stop someone inside the project from moving that data to another GCP location that you don’t control.

VPC Service Controls (VPC SC) allow you to define a security perimeter around your projects. Think of it as a firewall for Google APIs.

By placing our HPI project inside a VPC SC Perimeter and restricting the Cloud Storage and BigQuery APIs, we enforce the following:

  • No Egress to External Resources: A user cannot run a command like gsutil cp gs://my-corporate-hpi-data/file.csv gs://my-personal-bucket/. Even if the user has "Owner" permission on both buckets, VPC SC sees that the destination bucket lies outside the trusted perimeter and blocks the transfer.
  • You can ensure that data can only be accessed only from authorized corporate IP ranges or trusted devices, regardless of user credentials.

By combining these three tools, we have created a highly secure environment:

  • Automated by Assured Workloads.
  • Enforced by Organization Policies to be US-only.
  • Total clarity on who is reading patient data via Audit Logs.
  • API-level blocking of data movement outside the trusted boundary via VPC Service Controls.

This architecture moves beyond simple permissions and builds a structural fortress around your most sensitive healthcare data.

This Terraform code automates the entire architecture described above.

Prerequisites for running this Terraform:

  • You must have an Organization ID and Billing Account ID.
  • The account running Terraform needs highly privileged roles at the Organization level: roles/resourcemanager.organizationAdmin, roles/assuredworkloads.admin, and roles/accesscontextmanager.policyAdmin.
  • You must already have an Access Policy created at the Org level (VPC SC requires one). If you don’t, you need to create one first.
terraform {  required_providers {      source  = "hashicorp/google"    }}variable "org_id" {
  description = "The numeric Google Cloud Organization ID"
  type        = string
variable "billing_account_id" {
  description = "The billing account ID to attach to the project"}
variable "access_policy_id" {
  description = "The numeric ID of your existing Access Context Manager Policy (required for VPC SC)"  # Example format: "1234567890"provider "google" {
  # Credentials usually provided via GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS env var
}
# ============================================================================
# LAYER 1: Assured Workload (Creates the Folder & US-Only Policies)
# ============================================================================
resource "google_assured_workloads_workload" "hpi_us_workload" {
  organization = var.org_id
  location     = "us"
  display_name = "hpi-us-assured-folder"
  # This regime combines US residency requirements with Healthcare requirements
  compliance_regime = "US_REGIONAL_ACCESS" 
  # Note: Depending on your specific needs, you might select "HIPAA" or 
  # "HITRUST", but "US_REGIONAL_ACCESS" is the strictest for pure location.
  # The "Healthcare and Life Sciences Controls" package often runs on top of this.
  labels = {
    environment = "production"
    data_type   = "hpi"}
# ============================================================================
# The Project inside the Assured Workload Folder
# ============================================================================
resource "google_project" "hpi_project" {
  name       = "hpi-data-protected"
  project_id = "hpi-data-protected-${random_id.suffix.hex}"
  # Crucial: Place project inside the Assured Workload folder
  folder_id  = google_assured_workloads_workload.hpi_us_workload.id
  billing_account = var.billing_account_id
  # Enable necessary APIs for Health Data
  depends_on = [google_assured_workloads_workload.hpi_us_workload]
}
resource "google_project_service" "apis" {
  project = google_project.hpi_project.project_id    "compute.googleapis.com",
    "storage.googleapis.com",
    "bigquery.googleapis.com",
    "healthcare.googleapis.com"
  ])  disable_on_destroy = false
# ============================================================================
# LAYER 2: Enable Enhanced Audit Logging (Data Access Logs)
# ============================================================================
# We apply this at the Folder level so it inherits down to all projects within
resource "google_folder_iam_audit_config" "hpi_folder_audit" {
  folder  = google_assured_workloads_workload.hpi_us_workload.id
  service = "allServices" # Enable for BQ, GCS, etc.
  audit_log_config {
    log_type = "DATA_READ" # Log who reads the HPI data
  }    log_type = "DATA_WRITE" # Log who modifies data    audit_log_config {
    log_type = "ADMIN_READ" # Log metadata reads
  }# ============================================================================
# LAYER 3: VPC Service Controls Perimeter
# ============================================================================
resource "google_access_context_manager_service_perimeter" "hpi_perimeter" {
  parent       = "accessPolicies/${var.access_policy_id}"
  name         = "accessPolicies/${var.access_policy_id}/servicePerimeters/hpi_data_perimeter"
  title        = "HPI Data Protection Perimeter"
  perimeter_type = "PERIMETER_TYPE_REGULAR"
  status {
    # Define which projects are INSIDE the secure boundary
    resources = [
      "projects/${google_project.hpi_project.number}"
    ]
    # Define which services are restricted at the boundary.
    # Requests to these APIs involving data crossing the boundary will be blocked.      "storage.googleapis.com",
      "bigquery.googleapis.com",
      "healthcare.googleapis.com"    # Optional: Add Access Levels here to allow specific IPs/Users ingress
    # access_levels = [google_access_context_manager_access_level.trusted_corp_vpn.name]
  }
  # Ensure project exists before locking it down
  depends_on = [google_project.hpi_project]# Utility for project ID naming
resource "random_id" "suffix" {
  byte_length = 4

items.title: Building a Three-Tier Application with Kubernetes, Jenkins, and GitOps on AWS (Hands-On Project)
items.author: Mr. Kishor (Kishor Bhairat)
items.content:
From Docker and CI pipelines to Kubernetes on EC2 and Argo CD–based continuous deployment, with image scanning and Git-driven releases.

Over the last few days, I worked on a hands-on DevOps project focused on designing, deploying, and automating a three-tier application using modern cloud-native tools.

Instead of treating this as just an application deployment, my goal was to understand how different parts of the DevOps toolchain connect together — from containerization and CI pipelines to Kubernetes orchestration and GitOps-based deployment.

This article documents the , including:

  • Kubernetes cluster setup on AWS EC2
  • Docker and Docker Compose
  • CI with Jenkins, SonarQube, and Trivy
  • GitOps-based continuous deployment using Argo CD
  • Practical decisions around handling stateless vs stateful component

The application follows a classic :

    The application code was generated using , which allowed me to focus primarily on infrastructure, automation, and deployment workflows rather than application logic.

                    ┌──────────────┐                │   Browser    │                       │              ┌──────────────────┐              │   (React App)    │              └──────┬───────────┘                       ▼              │ Backend Service  │              │   Port: 5000     │                       │              ┌──────────────────┐              │   Port: 3306     │

    Each tier runs in its own container and is managed independently inside Kubernetes.

    Kubernetes Cluster Setup on AWS EC2

    Instead of using a managed service, I set up Kubernetes directly on AWS EC2 instances. This approach helped me understand Kubernetes fundamentals such as node roles, networking, and service discovery at a deeper level.

    Key aspects of the setup:

    • EC2 instances used as control plane and worker nodes
    • Kubernetes installed and configured manually
    • Cluster networking enabled for pod-to-pod and service-to-service communication

    This setup provided clear visibility into how Kubernetes operates behind the scenes.

    Containerization with Docker

    Separate were written for the frontend and backend services.

    • Frontend image builds the React application and serves it via a lightweight web server
    • Backend image packages the Node.js API along with its dependencies
    • Consistent runtime environments
    • Versioned, immutable images
    • Smooth integration with CI pipelines

    Local Validation Using Docker Compose

    Before deploying to Kubernetes, was used to validate the entire stack locally.

    Docker Compose helped to:

    • Run frontend, backend, and MySQL together
    • Verify environment variables and port mappings
    • Catch configuration issues early

    This step significantly reduced troubleshooting later in Kubernetes.

    Kubernetes Deployment Configuration

    Each component was deployed independently using Kubernetes:

    • Frontend Deployment and Service
    • Backend Deployment and Service
    • MySQL Deployment and Service
    • were used for non-sensitive configuration
    • were used for database credentials

    This separation keeps configuration manageable and avoids hardcoding sensitive values.

    Service Communication and Port Design

    Clear port separation was enforced:

    • Frontend → HTTP (e.g., 80)
    • Backend → API port (e.g., 5000)
    • MySQL → Database port (3306)

    Only the backend communicates with the database. The frontend never directly accesses MySQL, which avoids common architectural and security mistakes.

    A was implemented to validate changes before deployment.

    1. Static code analysis using
    2. Container image vulnerability scanning using 
    3. Image tagging and deployment configuration update

    This pipeline ensures that only validated and scanned images move forward.

    Image Tagging and Deployment Updates

    Instead of using the latest tag, every Docker image is built with a (for example, build number or commit ID).

    After successful validation:

    • Jenkins updates the image tag in the Kubernetes deployment manifests
    • The updated configuration is committed back to Git
    • Clear traceability between code and deployments
    • Better debugging visibility

    GitOps-Based Deployment with Argo CD

    Deployment is handled using with Argo CD.

    • are managed by Argo CD
    • from GitOps automation

    Databases are stateful and require careful handling. Excluding MySQL avoids unnecessary redeployments and aligns better with real-world deployment practices.

    Argo CD continuously monitors the Git repository and reconciles the Kubernetes cluster to match the declared state.

    🔄 Jenkins + Argo CD Pipeline Flow

           Developer            │        Git Push            ▼      │   Jenkins   │             │   │                   │SonarQube           Docker Build
    (Code Quality)      (Frontend & Backend)
       │                   │             ▼     (Image Vulnerabilities)             ▼ Kubernetes Deployment YAML             ▼             │      ┌─────────────┐      └──────┬──────┘             ▼     (Frontend & Backend)

    Separation of Responsibilities

    • Builds and validates artifacts
    • Runs quality and security checks
    • Updates deployment configuration in Git
    • Synchronizes Kubernetes state
    • Handles deployment reconciliation

    This separation keeps CI and CD concerns clearly defined.

    The repository is structured to clearly separate:

      This project helped me understand:

      • Kubernetes cluster setup on AWS EC2
      • Container lifecycle and image management
      • CI pipelines with integrated quality and security checks
      • GitOps workflows using Argo CD
      • Why stateless and stateful components are handled differently

      More importantly, it reinforced the difference between and designing systems thoughtfully.

      Building this project end-to-end provided hands-on experience across multiple layers of the DevOps toolchain. While there is always room for improvement, this setup helped me connect concepts like CI, container security, Kubernetes orchestration, and GitOps into a single workflow.

      Documenting the process through this article also helped clarify design decisions and highlight areas for future improvement.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.35: Introducing Workload Aware Scheduling
items.content:
Scheduling large workloads is a much more complex and fragile operation than scheduling a single Pod, as it often requires considering all Pods together instead of scheduling each one independently. For example, when scheduling a machine learning batch job, you often need to place each worker strategically, such as on the same rack, to make the entire process as efficient as possible. At the same time, the Pods that are part of such a workload are very often identical from the scheduling perspective, which fundamentally changes how this process should look.

There are many custom schedulers adapted to perform workload scheduling efficiently, but considering how common and important workload scheduling is to Kubernetes users, especially in the AI era with the growing number of use cases, it is high time to make workloads a first-class citizen for and support them natively.

Workload aware scheduling

The recent 1.35 release of Kubernetes delivered the first tranche of workload aware scheduling improvements. These are part of a wider effort that is aiming to improve scheduling and management of workloads. The effort will span over many SIGs and releases, and is supposed to gradually expand capabilities of the system toward reaching the north star goal, which is seamless workload scheduling and management in Kubernetes including, but not limited to, preemption and autoscaling.

Kubernetes v1.35 introduces the Workload API that you can use to describe the desired shape as well as scheduling-oriented requirements of the workload. It comes with an initial implementation of that instructs the to schedule gang Pods in the fashion. Finally, we improved scheduling of identical Pods (that typically make a gang) to speed up the process thanks to the feature.

The new Workload API resource is part of the scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha1API group. This resource acts as a structured, machine-readable definition of the scheduling requirements of a multi-Pod application. While user-facing workloads like Jobs define what to run, the Workload resource determines how a group of Pods should be scheduled and how its placement should be managed throughout its lifecycle.

A Workload allows you to define a group of Pods and apply a scheduling policy to them. Here is what a gang scheduling configuration looks like. You can define a named and apply the policy with a of 4.

When you create your Pods, you link them to this Workload using the new field:

How gang scheduling works

The policy enforces placement. Without gang scheduling, a Job might be partially scheduled, consuming resources without being able to run, leading to resource wastage and potential deadlocks.

When you create Pods that are part of a gang-scheduled pod group, the scheduler's plugin manages the lifecycle independently for each pod group (or replica key):

  1. When you create your Pods (or a controller makes them for you), the scheduler blocks them from scheduling, until:

    • The referenced Workload object is created.
    • The referenced pod group exists in a Workload.
    • The number of pending Pods in that group meets your .

  2. Once enough Pods arrive, the scheduler tries to place them. However, instead of binding them to nodes immediately, the Pods wait at a gate.

  3. The scheduler checks if it has found valid assignments for the entire group (at least the ).

    • If there is room for the group, the gate opens, and all Pods are bound to nodes.
    • If only a subset of the group pods was successfully scheduled within a timeout (set to 5 minutes), the scheduler rejects of the Pods in the group. They go back to the queue, freeing up the reserved resources for other workloads.

We'd like to point out that that while this is a first implementation, the Kubernetes project firmly intends to improve and expand the gang scheduling algorithm in future releases. Benefits we hope to deliver include a single-cycle scheduling phase for a whole gang, workload-level preemption, and more, moving towards the north star goal.

In addition to explicit gang scheduling, v1.35 introduces . This is a Beta feature that improves scheduling latency for identical Pods.

Unlike gang scheduling, this feature does not require the Workload API or any explicit opt-in on the user's part. It works opportunistically within the scheduler by identifying Pods that have identical scheduling requirements (container images, resource requests, affinities, etc.). When the scheduler processes a Pod, it can reuse the feasibility calculations for subsequent identical Pods in the queue, significantly speeding up the process.

Most users will benefit from this optimization automatically, without taking any special steps, provided their Pods meet the following criteria.

Opportunistic batching works under specific conditions. All fields used by the to find a placement must be identical between Pods. Additionally, using some features disables the batching mechanism for those Pods to ensure correctness.

Note that you may need to review your configuration to ensure it is not implicitly disabling batching for your workloads.

See the docs for more details about restrictions.

The project has a broad ambition to deliver workload aware scheduling. These new APIs and scheduling enhancements are just the first steps. In the near future, the effort aims to tackle:

  • Introducing a workload scheduling phase
  • Improved support for multi-node DRA and topology aware scheduling
  • Workload-level preemption
  • Improved integration between scheduling and autoscaling
  • Improved interaction with external workload schedulers
  • Managing placement of workloads throughout their entire lifecycle
  • Multi-workload scheduling simulations

And more. The priority and implementation order of these focus areas are subject to change. Stay tuned for further updates.

To try the workload aware scheduling improvements:

  • Workload API: Enable the feature gate on both and , and ensure the scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha1API group is enabled.
  • Gang scheduling: Enable the feature gate on (requires the Workload API to be enabled).
  • Opportunistic batching: As a Beta feature, it is enabled by default in v1.35. You can disable it using the feature gate on if needed.

We encourage you to try out workload aware scheduling in your test clusters and share your experiences to help shape the future of Kubernetes scheduling. You can send your feedback by:
