items.title: The Hidden Cost of Cheap Kubernetes Nodes — Complete Advanced Analysis with Real Metrics, Case…
items.author: M Mahdi Ramadhan, M. Si
items.content:
The Hidden Cost of Cheap Kubernetes Nodes — Complete Advanced Analysis with Real Metrics, Case Studies, and Excel Template

I really like Kubernetes, in the world of Kubernetes, the common assumption that cheaper nodes equal lower costs often leads teams to an unexpected paradox: the cheaper the node, the higher the bill. While smaller instances may seem economical at first glance, they can quietly inflate overall cluster expenses through hidden inefficiencies in pod density, networking limits, and control-plane overhead.

This article unifies both conceptual and advanced analytical views — combining cost economics, scheduling theory, and real command-line diagnostics — to help DevOps teams see beyond the illusion of cheap compute.

1. The Illusion of Savings

Most capacity planning focuses on . Yet Kubernetes scheduling operates under additional constraints — particularly , which is often capped by networking resources. On cloud providers such as AWS, smaller nodes (like t3.medium) come with fewer ENIs, fewer IPs per ENI, and hence fewer pods allowed per node.

Even when the CPU and RAM remain 50% free, the scheduler cannot place new pods once the IP ceiling is hit. It spins up new nodes, wasting compute and increasing cost.

From the outside, autoscaling appears healthy. In reality, you’re paying for idle CPU and RAM trapped by network limits.

2. Bigger Nodes, Smaller Bills

Larger nodes (m5.2xlarge, m5.4xlarge, etc.) allow significantly higher pod density, which reduces the number of total nodes required for the same workload. This minimizes control-plane interactions, ENI allocations, and log streams.

Although the larger node costs 9× more per hour, it delivers ~9× pod capacity and 3.6× higher efficiency per dollar.

3. Measuring Pod Density in Your Cluster

Before optimizing, measure where your cluster stands. Run these diagnostic commands:

# Pods per nodekubectl get pods -A -o wide --no-headers | awk '{print $8}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nrkubectl top nodes
# Max pod allocation per node type
kubectl describe node <node-name> | grep -i pods

Then compute :

kubectl get nodes -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ALLOC_PODS:.status.allocatable.pods

If running_pods / allocatable_pods < 0.7, you’re leaving capacity idle—either due to ENI/IP limits or uneven scheduling.

4. Quantifying the Control Plane Tax

Each node added increases control-plane load exponentially. Every new node introduces heartbeats, API calls, log streams, and metrics traffic. This overhead is invisible in your compute invoice but drains performance.

To inspect the control-plane tax:

# Count events per minutekubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | wc -l
# Measure API QPS trend (requires metrics-server)
kubectl get --raw "/metrics" | grep apiserver_request_total

Doubling node count does double control-plane load — it increases it geometrically.

5. Cost Efficiency Calculation: Real Example

Let’s calculate real pod-hour efficiency for AWS EKS using real-world pricing.

Scenario 1–4× m5.2xlarge nodes

Cost/day = 4 × 0.384 × 24 = $36.86Schedulable Pods = 58 × 4 = 232→ $36.86 / (232 × 0.99 × 24) ≈ $0.0069 per active pod-hour

Scenario 2–40× t3.medium nodes

Cost/day = 40 × 0.0416 × 24 = $39.94Schedulable Pods = 680 (65% real utilization)
→ $39.94 / (680 × 0.65 × 24) ≈ $0.010 per active pod-hour

The “” t3.medium setup costs ~45% more per effective pod-hour, due to fragmentation and density loss.

6. Understanding Engineering Overhead

When node count increases, your operational tax expands:

  • → Higher ingestion and storage bills.
  • → Higher deployment latency.
  • → Prometheus and Datadog cardinality spikes.
  • → Higher latency and debugging complexity.

This creates a  — SRE and DevOps engineers spend more time firefighting non-compute bottlenecks. These are invisible in billing but massive in total cost of ownership (TCO).

7. Advanced Cluster Diagnostic Checklist

Pair these with a Prometheus metric like kube_node_status_allocatable_pods to build a real-time efficiency dashboard.

8. Visualizing Real Cost Dynamics

When plotted, cost efficiency forms a U-curve:

  • Too-small nodes → Overhead dominates (fragmentation, IP exhaustion)
  • Too-large nodes → Risk of underutilization (wasted cores)
  • Optimal nodes → Maximize

The exact optimal point depends on your CNI, workload type, and deployment pattern. Generally, 2× or 4× vCPU nodes deliver the best cost-density balance.

9. The Formula for Real Efficiency

Real Efficiency = (Compute Utilization × Pod Density) / Control Plane Cost

This equation captures the essence of Kubernetes economics. When pod density or network availability drops, control-plane interactions spike. The system appears busy but accomplishes less work per dollar.

  1. Pod density is the hidden cost lever. Measure it regularly.
  2. ENI/IP limits are physical ceilings, not configuration errors.
  3. Cheap nodes multiply your control plane tax.
  4. is the only fair comparison metric.
  5. Optimize for utilization, not invoice price.

In Kubernetes, the cheapest node isn’t the one with the lowest hourly price — it’s the one that lets you do more work with fewer moving parts.

11. Excel Attachment: Cost Calculator (Formulas Included)

To make things easier for you, I’ve attached a simple Excel formula that I use for basic calculations when sizing cluster requirements. You can adjust or expand it as needed to fit your specific use case.

  • : currency, uptime ratio, and an instance catalog (type, vCPU, memory, max pods, price/hr).
  • : editable price and max-pod entries for your provider/region.
  • : scenario-based comparison with formulas for cost/day, active pod-hours/day, and cost per active pod-hour. Uses VLOOKUP into the catalog.
  • : per-node allocatable vs running pods, density ratio, and a flag when < 0.7.

Update and to fit your reality (e.g., different CNI density, spot pricing, or reserved instances). This lets you re-run the math in minutes and defend your node-sizing decision with hard numbers.

Kubernetes doesn’t charge you for inefficiency — but it makes you pay for it everywhere else: in autoscaling overhead, control-plane pressure, observability noise, and human time. When you treat node selection as a , not a , cost efficiency naturally follows.

Cheap nodes don’t save money; they redistribute it into invisible complexity.

items.title: The “Deploy on Friday” Confidence: Automating the Fear Out of DevOps
items.author: Hui Zhu
items.content:
It’s 4:55 PM on a Friday. A critical hotfix is ready. Your manager asks, “Can we ship this now?”

In most companies, this question triggers a panic attack. Engineers start sweating. “It’s too risky,” they say. “Let’s wait until Monday.” They know that deployment is a minefield of manual scripts, forgotten config variables, and “hope-based” engineering.

But in elite engineering teams, the answer is simply, “Done.”

They click a button, watch a green checkmark appear, and go home to their weekend.

The difference isn’t that they are smarter. It’s that they don’t trust humans to deploy software. They trust .

A robust CI/CD pipeline is the difference between “hero mode” (where one person knows how to deploy) and a scalable engineering culture. But building one from scratch — handling secrets, caching, security gates, and rollback strategies — is tedious work that often gets skipped.

I got tired of seeing fragile deploy scripts held together by duct tape. So I built the prompt. It creates production-grade automation workflows that make deploying software boring. And in DevOps, boring is beautiful.

The “Automation Architect” Prompt

This isn’t just about writing a YAML file. Anyone can copy-paste a basic GitHub Actions workflow.

This prompt acts as a . It forces you to think about the “unsexy” parts that actually matter:

  • : Blocking deployment if vulnerabilities are found.
  • : Caching layers so your devs aren’t waiting 20 minutes for a build.
  • : Knowing exactly how to undo a disaster before it starts.

Copy this instruction into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini to generate your pipeline.

# Role DefinitionYou are a Senior DevOps Architect and CI/CD Specialist with 10+ years of experience designing and implementing enterprise-grade automation pipelines. You have deep expertise in:
- Pipeline orchestration tools (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, CircleCI)- Container orchestration (Docker, Kubernetes, Helm)
- Infrastructure as Code (Terraform, Pulumi, CloudFormation)
- Security scanning and compliance automation (SAST, DAST, SCA)
- Multi-environment deployment strategies (Blue-Green, Canary, Rolling)
- Observability and monitoring integration
# Task DescriptionDesign and optimize a CI/CD pipeline based on the provided project requirements. Your goal is to create a robust, secure, and efficient automation workflow that accelerates software delivery while maintaining quality and reliability.
Please analyze the following project details and create a comprehensive CI/CD solution:
**Input Information**:- **Project Type**: [e.g., microservices, monolith, serverless, mobile app]
- **Tech Stack**: [e.g., Node.js, Python, Java, Go, React]
- **Deployment Target**: [e.g., AWS EKS, GCP GKE, Azure AKS, bare metal]
- **Team Size**: [number of developers]
- **Current Pain Points**: [manual deployments, slow builds, lack of testing, etc.]
- **Security Requirements**: [compliance standards, security scanning needs]
- **Existing Tools**: [current CI/CD tools, if any]
## 1. Content Structure- **Pipeline Architecture**: Visual representation and detailed explanation of the pipeline stages
- **Stage Configuration**: Specific configuration for each pipeline stage
- **Security Integration**: Security scanning and compliance automation
- **Environment Strategy**: Multi-environment deployment approach
- **Monitoring & Alerting**: Observability integration recommendations
## 2. Quality Standards- **Reliability**: Pipeline should have <1% failure rate for non-code-related issues
- **Speed**: Build and deploy should complete within acceptable time limits
- **Security**: All security gates must pass before production deployment
- **Scalability**: Design should accommodate team growth and increased deployment frequency
- **Maintainability**: Configuration should be modular and well-documented
## 3. Format Requirements- Provide pipeline configuration in YAML format (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or requested tool)
- Include inline comments explaining each step
- Provide a pipeline diagram using Mermaid or ASCII art
- List all required secrets and environment variables
- Include rollback procedures
## 4. Style Constraints- **Language Style**: Technical but accessible, avoiding unnecessary jargon
- **Expression**: Direct and actionable with clear reasoning
- **Depth**: Deep technical detail with practical implementation guidance
Before delivering, verify:- [ ] Pipeline covers all stages: build, test, security scan, deploy, verify
- [ ] Secrets management is properly addressed
- [ ] Rollback strategy is clearly defined
- [ ] Pipeline is optimized for speed (parallel jobs, caching)
- [ ] Security scanning is integrated at appropriate stages
- [ ] Environment-specific configurations are separated
- [ ] Monitoring and alerting hooks are included
- [ ] Documentation for maintenance and troubleshooting is provided
# Important Notes- Always use locked/pinned versions for actions and dependencies
- Never expose secrets in logs or artifacts
- Implement proper branch protection and approval workflows
- Consider cost implications for cloud-based runners
- Design for idempotency - pipelines should be safely re-runnable
# Output FormatProvide the complete CI/CD solution in the following structure:
1. Executive Summary (2-3 sentences)
2. Pipeline Architecture Diagram
3. Complete Pipeline Configuration (YAML)
4. Stage-by-Stage Explanation
5. Security Considerations
6. Environment Variables and Secrets List8. Optimization Recommendations
9. Maintenance Guidelines

Why This Prompt Delivers “Sleep-at-Night” Quality

Most CI/CD tutorials show you the “Happy Path”: Code compiles -> Code pushes. Real life is rarely that simple. This prompt forces the AI to handle the messy reality.

1. It Demands a “Security Sandwich”

Notice the requirements around . The prompt ensures your pipeline isn’t just a delivery mechanism; it’s a checkpoint. It injects steps for SAST (Static Application Security Testing) and dependency scanning the build, and container scanning the build. It stops you from shipping a vulnerability just because you were in a rush.

2. It Prioritizes Speed (Because Slow Pipelines Kill Culture)

If a pipeline takes 45 minutes, developers stop running it. They start bypassing checks. The prompt explicitly asks for Optimization Recommendations like parallel jobs and aggressive caching. It pushes the AI to design a workflow that respects your time.

Things break. The Rollback Procedures requirement is critical. When a bad deploy happens (and it will), you shouldn't be frantically Googling "how to revert helm chart." The prompt forces the AI to document the "Undo" button before you ever hit "Do".

: Node.js API, deploying to AWS ECS.
Result: You’ll get a GitHub Actions workflow that runs npm test across a matrix of Node versions, builds the container, scans it for CVEs, and updates your ECS service—all in under 5 minutes.

For a Compliance Heavy Project:

: SOC 2 compliance, audit logs required.
Result: The prompt will generate a pipeline with manual approval gates for production, artifact signing, and automated log shipping to your audit trail.

For Cleaning Up a Messy Jenkinsfile:

: Jenkins server crashes, builds take 1 hour.
Result: It will suggest a modernized architecture, perhaps moving to ephemeral runners or splitting the build into parallel stages to cut that hour down to 15 minutes.

Stop treating your pipeline as an afterthought. It is the factory floor of your software. If the factory floor is chaotic, the product will be flawed. Use this prompt to build a machine that works so reliably, you can forget it’s even there.

And yes, even deploy on Fridays.

items.title: Building a Production-Ready CI/CD Pipeline with GitHub Actions and ArgoCD
items.author: Sachin Lakshan
items.content:
Modern apps move fast — and if your deployment process doesn’t, you’ll feel that pain pretty quickly 😅

That’s where a solid CI/CD pipeline comes in.

In this post, we’ll build a fully automated, production-ready CI/CD pipeline for a application using:

  • for Continuous Integration
  • for GitOps-based Continuous Deployment
  • Kind (Kubernetes in Docker) for a local Kubernetes cluster

By the end, you’ll have a setup where:

  • Code changes go through quality checks automatically
  • Docker images are built and versioned
  • Deployments happen without running a single kubectl apply
  • Kubernetes stays perfectly in sync with Git (thanks to GitOps ✨)
  • GitHub Actions builds, tests, and ships Docker images
  • ArgoCD watches Git and deploys automatically

Once this is set up, merging a PR is all it takes to deploy your app.

🧰 Prerequisites (Don’t Panic)

You don’t need to be an expert in everything listed here. If you’ve touched most of these tools before, you’re good.

  • Homebrew (or your OS package manager)

Knowledge (at a high level)

  • Basic Git & GitHub workflows
  • React + TypeScript basics
  • Kubernetes concepts (pods, deployments, services)

👉 If any of this sounds fuzzy — don’t worry. I’ll explain things as we go.

🔁 GitOps (In Plain English)

Before diving into the actual setup, let’s first talk GitOps — because this is the heart of the whole setup.

GitOps means Git is the source of truth.If it’s not in Git, it shouldn’t exist in your cluster.

Instead of running deployment commands manually:

  • You change something in Git
  • Kubernetes updates itself automatically

No SSH. No kubectl apply. No guessing.

  • ✅ Every deployment is a Git commit
  • 🤝 Better collaboration with PRs and reviews
  • 🛠 Drift detection (manual changes get reverted automatically)

We’ll use :

    ⚠️ This post is about DevOps, not frontend development, so I won’t walk through every React app file. Trust me — explaining every React app file here would turn this post into a novel 📖😅

      When CI builds a new image, it updates the manifest repo, and ArgoCD takes it from there.

      Don’t worry if these concepts aren’t clicking . By the time we finish the full setup, it’ll all make sense. 😄

      So, let’s move on to the !

      ☸️ Setting Up a Local Kubernetes Cluster with Kind

      Alright, before we jump into the actual setup, let’s quickly talk about why we’re using Kind for this tutorial.

      Sure, you could go with Minikube, or even spin up a cloud cluster using GKE/EKS/AKS. But honestly:

      1.  — I don’t want you paying for a cluster just to follow along. Kind runs everything in Docker on your laptop, so it’s free.
      2.  — Not everyone has a super-powered machine. Minikube can get heavy and slow on low-spec laptops. Kind is super lightweight and zippy, so almost anyone can run it.

      Basically, Kind gives us a real-ish Kubernetes cluster locally without breaking the bank or your CPU fan 😄.

      Cool — now let’s spin up our cluster!

      We’ll create a and expose our app via NodePort.

      You can create the following YAML file  — it doesn’t have to live inside your project repo. Once the file is ready, we’ll use it to spin up the Kind cluster with a single command.

      Here’s the cluster configuration (cluster-config.yaml):

      kind: ClusterapiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4- role: control-plane  - containerPort: 30007    protocol: TCP- role: worker
      kind create cluster --name dev-cluster --config cluster-config.yaml

      You should see 1 control-plane + 2 workers, all ready.

      Awesome! Our local Kubernetes cluster is up and running. 🎉

      Now, let’s move on to and getting it ready for deployment. 🐳

      🐳 Containerizing the App (Multi-Stage Dockerfile)

      Alright, before we dive into the Dockerfile, I have one thing to mention very clearly: this section assumes you already know the basics of Docker — what images and containers are, and how a Dockerfile works. If you don’t, you might get lost halfway through.

      No worries though — I wrote a full Docker guide that covers all the essentials. You can check it out here. I highly recommend reading it first before moving on, so the docker images and pushing them to Docker Hub make total sense. Trust me, it’ll save you a lot of head-scratching later 😄.

      With that out of the way, let’s see the setup now.

      We’re using a .

      • Stage 1 → Build React app with Node
      • Stage 2 → Serve it with Nginx

      Why? Smaller images, faster deploys, smaller attack surface.

      # Stage 1: Build the applicationFROM node:22.16.0-alpine AS builder
      RUN corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@10.25.0 --activate
      WORKDIR /app
      COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml ./
      RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfileRUN pnpm run build
      # Stage 2: Serve with Nginx
      FROM nginx:1.28.0-alpine
      COPY --from=builder /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
      COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.confCMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
      “Cool… but how does this actually become a Docker image and reach Docker Hub?”

      🏗️ Building the Docker Image Locally

      From the root of your React project, run:

      docker build -t <dockerhub-username>/reactapp-ui:local .

      You should see something like:

      🔐 Logging in to Docker Hub

      If you haven’t authenticated already, you’ll be prompted for:

      • Docker Hub password (recommended)

      Once authenticated, Docker can push images on your behalf.

      🚀 Pushing the Image to Docker Hub

      docker push <dockerhub-username>/reactapp-ui:local
      • The image is publicly available on Docker Hub
      • Any Kubernetes cluster — local or cloud — can pull it
      • This is the exact same flow CI will automate later

      👉 At this point, you already have:

      • A production-ready Nginx setup
      • A deployable artifact for Kubernetes

      💡 Now you might be thinking, “Wait, why are we manually creating the Docker image and pushing it to Docker Hub? Didn’t you say CI would do that?”

      Good question! Here’s the deal: if we don’t push the image manually for the first time, ArgoCD won’t be able to pull it when we set it up. That means your app will show as , which isn’t ideal for the first impression.

      If this doesn’t fully click right now, don’t worry — once we set up ArgoCD, it’ll all make sense. 😄

      Great! Our app is now nicely packaged into a Docker image and ready to go. ✅

      Next up, let’s dive into the and see how we automate building, testing, and deploying our app. 🚀

      🏗 CI/CD Architecture (High Level)

      Before we look at the CI/CD pipeline setup and its architecture, let’s quickly align on what CI/CD means in this setup. CI (Continuous Integration) validates every change by running checks, building the app, and packaging it into a Docker image that gets pushed to an artifact registry like Docker Hub. CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment), in a GitOps model, is handled by ArgoCD and simply means deploying the built and packaged application to Kubernetes based on what’s defined in Git. Together, this gives us a clean flow from in the cluster, without manual deployment steps.

      Here’s how everything connects:

      • PR opened → lint, test, build
      • PR merged → build Docker image
      • Update Kubernetes manifests in Git
      • Watches the manifest repo
      • Detects image tag changes
      • Syncs Kubernetes automatically
      • Keeps cluster state aligned with Git

      Once this is up & running, deployments become boring — and that’s a good thing 😌

      Before we look at the actual workflows, let’s quickly talk about , some common terms, and why we’re using it instead of Jenkins.

      is GitHub’s built-in automation platform. It lets you run workflows when certain events happen in your repo — like opening a pull request or pushing code to main. In this project, we use it to lint, test, package our app into a Docker image, push it to Docker Hub, and kick off the GitOps flow.

      You might be wondering: Honestly, Jenkins is powerful, but it comes with a lot of baggage — managing servers, plugins, credentials, upgrades… not fun. GitHub Actions is already integrated with GitHub, requires zero infrastructure setup, and is more than enough for most CI/CD use cases. Less setup, less maintenance, more time shipping code 🚀.

      Another big win with GitHub Actions is . Instead of writing everything from scratch, you can reuse community-maintained actions like:

      • actions/checkout to pull your code
      • actions/setup-node to install Node.js
      • docker/build-push-action to build and push images

      This makes workflows shorter, cleaner, and way easier to understand.

      There are few key terms you’ll keep seeing when working with GitHub actions:

      •  — An automated process (defined in YAML) that runs when a specific event happens.
      •  — A group of steps that run on the same runner.
      •  — A single task, like installing dependencies or running tests.
      •  — The machine that executes the workflow (GitHub provides these for free).

      All workflows live inside the .github/workflows directory in the repo.

      In this project, we use :

      • pull-request.yaml → Runs quality checks on pull requests
      • ci.yaml → Runs after merging to main, builds the Docker image, pushes it to Docker Hub, and updates the GitOps repo

      Alright, with that context out of the way, let’s look at how these workflows are set up.

      All run in parallel → fast feedback.

      Once branch protection is enabled, no PR can be merged unless all checks pass.

      This is your quality gate.

      name: PR Quality Check  pull_request:  lint:    steps:        uses: actions/checkout@v5        uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4          version: 10.25.0        uses: actions/setup-node@v6          node-version: 22
            - name: Install dependencies
              run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile        run: pnpm run lint    runs-on: ubuntu-latest      - name: Checkout code
              uses: actions/checkout@v5
              uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4          version: 10.25.0        uses: actions/setup-node@v6          node-version: 22
            - name: Install dependencies
              run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile        run: pnpm run test    runs-on: ubuntu-latest      - name: Checkout code
              uses: actions/checkout@v5
              uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4          version: 10.25.0        uses: actions/setup-node@v6          node-version: 22
            - name: Install dependencies
              run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile        run: pnpm run build

      🔒 Enabling Branch Protection Rules

      This is  — without it, these CI checks are just suggestions.

      1. Go to Repo → Settings → Branches
      2. Click Add branch protection rule
      • ✅ Require pull request before merging
      • ✅ Require status checks to pass
      • Add: lint, test, build (⚠️ These names must exactly match your job names in the workflow file. GitHub will only show these options after the workflow has run at least once, so make sure you’ve created at least one PR that triggers the workflow.)
      • ✅ Require branch to be up to date
      • ✅ Do not allow bypassing rules (recommended)
      • The button stays disabled until checks pass
      • Quality becomes non-negotiable 💪

      This workflow runs .

      • Pushes image to Docker Hub
      • Updates Kubernetes manifests repo

      This last step is the  — the magic moment ✨

      name: CI Pipelineon:    branches: [main]  build-and-push:    steps:        uses: actions/checkout@v5        uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4          version: 10.25.0        uses: actions/setup-node@v6          node-version: 22
            - name: Install dependencies
              run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile        run: pnpm run lint        run: pnpm run test        run: pnpm run build
            - name: Login to Docker Hub
              uses: docker/login-action@v3          username: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_USERNAME }}
                password: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_TOKEN }}
              uses: docker/build-push-action@v5          push: true
                tags: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_USERNAME }}/reactapp-ui:${{ github.sha }}
            # The GitOps "Magic" Step: Update the separate manifest repo
            - name: Update Manifest Repo          git config --global user.email "ci@example.com"
                git config --global user.name "CI-Bot"
                git clone https://${{ secrets.GH_PAT }}@github.com/gpslakshan/reactapp-manifests.git          # Use 'sed' to update the image tag in your deployment file
                sed -i "s|image: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_USERNAME }}/reactapp-ui:.*|image: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_USERNAME }}/reactapp-ui:${{ github.sha }}|g" deployment.yaml
                git add deployment.yaml
                git commit -m "Update image to ${{ github.sha }}"
                git push

      🔐 Secrets You’ll Need (Step-by-Step)

      Go to: GitHub Repo → Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions

      • Docker Hub → Account Settings →
      • Create token with
      • Paste it into GitHub secrets
      • GitHub → Settings → Developer Settings
      • Personal access tokens →
      • Repository access:
      • Permissions: Contents →

      This allows CI to update deployment.yaml.

      At this point, our CI setup looks solid — but the pipeline won’t work end to end just yet. We haven’t set up the Kubernetes manifests or ArgoCD, so there’s nothing actually deploying our app right now.

      No worries though — that’s exactly what we’re doing next. Let’s move forward and complete the remaining pieces of the puzzle. 🚀

      📄 Kubernetes Manifests (GitOps Repo)

      Before we jump into the Kubernetes manifests (YAML files), a quick heads-up: you should have a basic understanding of Kubernetes (k8s) to make sense of these manifests. If you’re new to k8s, I highly suggest getting familiar with the basics first. There are tons of tutorials online, or you can even ask an AI to guide you through the fundamentals — whatever works best for you.

      That said, I’ll briefly cover the core Kubernetes concepts relevant to this tutorial (like I promised at the start) so you’re not totally lost. There are you need to understand:

      1.  — The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. Think of it like a container, but really it’s a wrapper around one or more containers.
      2.  — We don’t create pods directly because that would mean missing out on the magic of Kubernetes: auto-healing, auto-scaling, and rolling updates. Deployments handle all that for us, which is why they’re necessary.
      3.  — A replica is just an identical pod. ReplicaSets manage those replicas. But don’t worry — you won’t touch them directly; they’re automatically created and managed by the Deployment.
      4.  — Exposes your app to the outside world. There are different types (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer), but for this tutorial we’ll keep it simple. You can explore these types on your own if you want.

      💡 If you’d like me to write a , let me know in the comments of this blog post — I’d love to put one together for you.

      apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: reactapp-ui  replicas: 3    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:      - name: reactapp-ui
              image: sachinlakshan/reactapp-ui:local # CI will overwrite this
              ports:

      Our deployment manifest defines how Kubernetes should run our application. We specify three replicas for high availability and load distribution. If one pod fails, the other two continue serving traffic. Three replicas also allow for zero-downtime rolling updates — Kubernetes can update one pod at a time while the others handle requests.

      The container image references our Docker Hub image, and the CI pipeline automatically updates this reference whenever new code is pushed. The container exposes port 80, which matches the port Nginx listens on inside our container.

      apiVersion: v1kind: Service  name: react-app-service  type: NodePort    app: reactapp-ui # This MUST match the label in your deployment.yaml    - protocol: TCP
            port: 80        # The port the Service listens on
            targetPort: 80  # The port your Docker container is listening on
            nodePort: 30007 # A fixed port to access it via your cluster nodes

      The service manifest creates a stable network endpoint for accessing our application. We use a NodePort service type, which is appropriate for local development and exposes the application on a fixed port across all cluster nodes.

      ⚠️ Critical Configuration: The selector must exactly match the labels in the deployment manifest. This is how Kubernetes knows which pods should receive traffic from this service. The service listens on port 80 and forwards to the container’s port 80. The nodePort 30007 is exposed on the cluster nodes and mapped to through our Kind configuration which we setup previously. In other words, nodePort Must Match the Kind Port Mapping.

      Kind extraPortMappings.containerPort == Service.nodePort

      🤔 “Wait… Aren’t We Running kubectl apply?”

      At this point, a very natural thought might pop into your head:

      “We have deployment.yaml and service.yaml… why aren’t we just running kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml -f service.yaml right now?”

      Totally fair question 😄That’s how most of us learned Kubernetes.

      But here’s the short answer:

      👉 That’s none of our business.

      😎 Why We’re Not Using kubectl apply?

      • Developers deploy to Kubernetes
      • CI deploy to Kubernetes
      • Humans deploy to Kubernetes

        Once the manifests are in Git:

        • watches the repository
        • applies them to the cluster
        kubectl apply -f deployment.yamlkubectl apply -f service.yaml

        And then we grab a coffee ☕️

        🧠 Why This Is Actually a Big Deal?

        This approach gives us some powerful guarantees:

        • ✅ Every deployment is traceable to a Git commit
        • ✅ No “who ran what command?” mystery
        • ✅ No config drift from manual changes
        • ✅ Easy rollbacks (git revert → ArgoCD handles the rest)

        If something exists in the cluster: 👉 It exist in Git 👉 Otherwise, ArgoCD will delete or fix it

        • They live in the
        • Tell it which repo to watch
        • And let it do what it does best — 

        From this point onward, Kubernetes is , not ours 😌

        Now that we’ve been chanting long enough, it’s time to actually  😄

        • CI has been building images
        • CI has been updating the manifest repo
        • Kubernetes has been patiently waiting

        Now we introduce the final boss: 👉 ArgoCD — the thing that actually deploys

        📦 Step 1: Installing ArgoCD into the Cluster

        First, we install ArgoCD .

        kubectl create namespace argocd
        kubectl apply -n argocd \  -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml

        This installs everything ArgoCD needs:

          kubectl get pods -n argocd

          ✅ Wait until before moving on.

          🌐 Step 2: Accessing the ArgoCD UI

          By default, ArgoCD isn’t exposed outside the cluster.For local development, we’ll use .

          kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd 8080:443

          Your browser will complain about SSL — that’s expected.Click .

          🔑 Step 3: Logging into ArgoCD

            kubectl -n argocd get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret \  -o jsonpath="{.data.password}" | base64 -d

            Log in and change the password immediately:

            • Top-right → User Info → Update Password

            📄 Step 4: Creating the ArgoCD Application

            Now we tell ArgoCD what to deploy and from where.

            • reactapp-ui

            ⚡ Step 5: Watching ArgoCD Do Its Thing

            As soon as you click Create:

            • ArgoCD pulls the manifest repo
            • Reads deployment.yaml and service.yaml
            • Applies them to the cluster

              If something’s wrong, ArgoCD tells you what and why — no guessing.

              🔍 Step 6: Verifying the Deployment (Kubernetes Side)

              Let’s double-check from the terminal.

              You should see  running.

              kubectl get svc react-app-service

                🌍 Step 7: Access the Application

                🎉 Your React app is now running in Kubernetes — deployed via GitOps.

                No kubectl apply.No manual steps.

                Step 8: Proving the Entire Pipeline Works

                Now for the fun part — let’s prove this pipeline actually works end-to-end.

                git checkout -b feature/update-versiongit commit -am "Update version to 1.3"

                What Happens Next (Automatically)?

                1. Lint, Test, Build
                2. Branch protection blocks merge if anything fails
                3. Builds Docker image -> Tags it with commit SHA -> Pushes to Docker Hub -> Updates manifest repo)
                4. ArgoCD detects the change
                5. Kubernetes performs a

                🔎 Step 9: Final Verification

                  From commit → production-like deploymentWith zero manual intervention.

                  🧘 Final Thoughts (Yes, It’s Actually This Simple)

                  • Kubernetes is managed by ArgoCD
                  • Deployments are managed by Git
                  • Humans are safely removed from the deployment path 😄

                  This is , not just theory.

                  You’ve built something that:

                  • Scales to production (Swap Kind for EKS/GKE/AKS and you’re basically there)
                  • Mirrors how modern DevOps teams operate

                  And most importantly: 👉 You understand it end-to-end

                  Thanks a lot for sticking till the end! I know this was a long post, but I hope it gave you a clear and practical understanding of how a modern CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions, GitOps, ArgoCD, and Kubernetes actually works.

                  If you found this post helpful,  — it really helps the post reach more developers who might benefit from it.And if you have any questions, ideas, or want to see a next, drop a comment below. I’d love to hear from you!

                  Happy coding and may your deployments always be green! 🚀

items.title: Designing Smoke Tests for APIs: A Production-Grade FastAPI Load Testing Setup
items.author: Okan Yenigün
items.content:
FastAPI Smoke Testing in Practice: k6, Nginx, Prometheus, and Grafana

In this article, we build a minimal yet production-shaped load-testing environment to demonstrate how modern API systems can be tested, observed, and reasoned about under load.

In my previous post on the subject, I conceptually introduced load tests.

In this chapter, we will begin by applying a smoke test to an API.

You can get the project code from here.

Remember, smoke tests ask a simple question:

To answer this question, we will design a small but realistic system composed of an API, infrastructure dependencies, a reverse proxy, a metrics pipeline, and a load generator.

Each component is deliberately chosen to mirror real-world architecture while remaining simple enough to understand end-to-end.

At the center of the system is a that exposes several types of endpoints.

# app/main.pyimport osimport mathfrom fastapi import FastAPI, Queryimport redis
from prometheus_fastapi_instrumentator import Instrumentator
app = FastAPI(title="Load Testing Demo API", version="0.4.1")
# ✅ Instrumentation must happen before the app starts
instrumentator = Instrumentator()
instrumentator.instrument(app)
instrumentator.expose(app, endpoint="/metrics", include_in_schema=False)
def get_db_dsn() -> str | None:
    return os.getenv("DATABASE_URL")
def get_redis_url() -> str | None:
    return os.getenv("REDIS_URL")    dsn = get_db_dsn()        raise RuntimeError("DATABASE_URL is not set")
    with psycopg.connect(dsn, connect_timeout=2) as conn:
        with conn.cursor() as cur:            cur.fetchone()
def check_redis() -> None:
    url = get_redis_url()        raise RuntimeError("REDIS_URL is not set")
    r = redis.Redis.from_url(url, socket_connect_timeout=2, socket_timeout=2)
    if r.ping() is not True:
        raise RuntimeError("redis ping failed")
def healthz():def readyz():        check_db()        return {"status": "not-ready", "reason": f"db_error: {type(e).__name__}"}        check_redis()        return {"status": "not-ready", "reason": f"redis_error: {type(e).__name__}"}
    return {"status": "ready"}
def catalog():    if not url:
        return {"status": "error", "reason": "REDIS_URL is not set"}
    r = redis.Redis.from_url(url, socket_connect_timeout=2, socket_timeout=2)
    cached = r.get(key)        payload = json.loads(cached)        return payload        "items": [
            {"sku": "A-001", "name": "Widget", "price": 19.9},
            {"sku": "B-002", "name": "Gadget", "price": 29.9},
            {"sku": "C-003", "name": "Doohickey", "price": 9.9},
        ],
        "generated_at": time.time(),
        "cache": "miss",
    r.setex(key, 30, json.dumps(payload))
    return payloaddef work(ms: int = Query(20, ge=0, le=2000)):    Deterministic workload endpoint.
    ms controls simulated work duration.
    """    return {"status": "ok", "work_ms": ms}def cpu(ms: int = Query(10, ge=0, le=2000)):    CPU-bound endpoint: burns CPU for ~ms milliseconds.    end = time.perf_counter() + (ms / 1000.0)    while time.perf_counter() < end:
        # do some floating point work
        x = math.sqrt(x + 123.456)  # arbitrary
    return {"status": "ok", "cpu_ms": ms, "result": x}
  • Health and readiness endpoints (/healthz, /readyz) to model Kubernetes-style lifecycle checks
  • A (/catalog) that simulates real request paths involving Redis
  • A deterministic latency endpoint (/work) for controlled response-time testing
  • A (/cpu) to study compute saturation behavior

The API is instrumented using from the very beginning. Metrics are exposed via /metrics and scraped continuously, ensuring that every request made during testing is observable.

//app/requirements.txtuvicorn[standard]==0.35.0redis==5.2.1
prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator==7.1.0

The API does not run in isolation. It is deployed together with:

  • , used by readiness checks to validate database connectivity
  • , used both for readiness checks and as a real cache dependency
  • , acting as a reverse proxy and traffic entry point, enabling realistic request routing, connection handling, and upstream behavior
  • , responsible for scraping and storing application metrics
  • , used to visualize request rates, error rates, and latency percentiles
  • , used as a programmable load generator to execute controlled test scenarios

All services are orchestrated using , creating a reproducible and isolated environment that behaves similarly to a small production deployment.

# docker-compose.yml  api:    container_name: demo_api      - "8000:8000"      - TZ=Europe/Istanbul
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://app:app@db:5432/appdb
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0      db:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "python -c \"import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:8000/healthz').read()\""]
      interval: 5s      retries: 10      resources:           cpus: "1.0"    command: >      --host 0.0.0.0      --workers 4    image: postgres:16    environment:      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=app    ports:    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U app -d appdb -h 127.0.0.1"]
      interval: 5s      retries: 20      resources:          cpus: "1.0"    image: redis:7-alpine
    container_name: demo_redis
    ports:    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s      retries: 20      resources:          cpus: "0.5"    image: nginx:1.27-alpine
    container_name: demo_nginx
    depends_on:        condition: service_healthy      - "8080:80"      - ./nginx/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro
      - ./nginx/conf.d:/etc/nginx/conf.d:ro
    deploy:        limits:          memory: 256M    image: prom/prometheus:v2.54.1
    container_name: demo_prometheus
    ports:    volumes:
      - ./observability/prometheus/prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml:ro
    command:
      - "--config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml"
    depends_on:        condition: service_healthy      resources:          cpus: "0.5"    image: grafana/grafana:11.2.2
    container_name: demo_grafana
    ports:    environment:
      - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_USER=admin
      - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin      prometheus:
        condition: service_started
    deploy:        limits:          memory: 512M    image: grafana/k6:0.54.0    depends_on:        condition: service_started      - ./loadtests/k6:/scripts:ro
    entrypoint: ["sleep", "infinity"]

Using k6, we generate a small number of concurrent users hitting core endpoints. During these runs, we validate:

  • All endpoints return successful responses
  • No unexpected 4xx or 5xx errors occur
  • Latency remains stable and well below defined thresholds
  • Traffic patterns in Grafana match the test definitions
  • Prometheus metrics reflect exactly what is happening at runtime
k6 ↓ ↓ ↓        ↓
FastAPI → Prometheus → Grafana

This approach ensures that the load-testing setup itself is correct before attempting any form of capacity or stress testing.

//loadtests/k6/smoke.js
import http from "k6/http";
import { check, sleep } from "k6";  vus: 2,  thresholds: {
    http_req_failed: ["rate<0.01"],      // <1% errors
    http_req_duration: ["p(95)<250"],    // p95 < 250ms (for this tiny smoke)};
const BASE_URL = __ENV.BASE_URL || "http://nginx";
export default function () {
  const res1 = http.get(`${BASE_URL}/healthz`);
  check(res1, { "healthz 200": (r) => r.status === 200 });
  const res2 = http.get(`${BASE_URL}/readyz`);
  check(res2, { "readyz 200": (r) => r.status === 200 });
  const res3 = http.get(`${BASE_URL}/catalog`);
  check(res3, {
    "catalog 200": (r) => r.status === 200,
    "catalog has cache field": (r) => {        return JSON.parse(r.body).cache !== undefined;        return false;    },}

vus: 2 → only two virtual users → minimal pressure.

duration: "20s" → Short run, just enough to exercise endpoints.

http_req_failed: rate < 0.01 → less than .

http_req_duration: p(95) < 250ms → sanity-level latency expectation.

👉 if smoke.js fails, baseline, load, or stress tests.

  • for metrics
  • A that exists only to run load tests

Let’s run a very small, safe load (2 VUs, 20s) against the API through NGINX, just to confirm the system is alive, wired correctly, and returning valid responses.

docker exec -it demo_k6 k6 run /scripts/smoke.js
“Send a very small number of requests to the system to verify that everything works.”
k6 → nginx → api → redis/postgres

This smoke test result says: our whole stack is healthy and responding fast, and the smoke test constraints were met.

VUs = Virtual Users. We ran 2 users in parallel. They looped for 20 seconds. “looping” means each VU repeatedly runs the default function in smoke.js:

    So one loop = 3 HTTP requests + 1 second pause.

    ✓ healthz 200✓ readyz 200✓ catalog has cache field

    160 checks total: script has 4 checks per iteration (healthz, readyz, catalog status, catalog cache-field).
